parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values on red and blue chips, with common-value green chips. Bear strongly values blue ($1) and moderately values red ($0.60). Buffalo and Goose both value blue only modestly (Buffalo $0.70, Goose $0.10) and red very little ($0.10). All value green at $0.50. Public information reveals that green is exactly $0.50 for everyone, but red and blue are only known privately, creating uncertainty about who values which color most. Players know their own valuations, see others’ chip holdings and trade history, and can compute their own surplus for any proposed trade, but cannot directly see others’ values.

Given these valuations, Bear is the likely high-value buyer of blue and a relatively higher-value holder of red compared to the others. Buffalo’s best opportunities are to convert low-value red (for Buffalo) into blue or green at reasonable terms, and to avoid giving away blue too cheaply. Goose, who values both red and blue at only $0.10, is nearly indifferent between red and blue in private value and will mainly look to gain disposable chips (especially green) or risk-free improvements while avoiding clear losses. Because everyone knows that green is worth $0.50 to all, green trades are focal and low-ambiguity: exchanging green against red or blue will be hard unless the other side believes the color they get is higher private value.

The core strategic uncertainty is about the private values of red and blue. Bear knows that blue is extremely valuable for herself and will try to acquire it cheaply, but doesn’t know others’ valuations. Buffalo knows red is almost worthless and will try to sell red at a gain, but cannot tell whether Bear or Goose values red more. Goose sees both red and blue as almost worthless and may be reluctant to part with green, but may accept offers that increase her total chip count if losses are small or zero.

Behaviorally, all three players show relatively low to medium ultimatum rejection thresholds and moderate fairness norms. That suggests they are not extremely aggressive; they will look for clearly positive-sum or at least non-exploitative deals, but are unlikely to reject small positive gains out of spite. With only three rounds and one proposal each per round, time is limited: players have mild incentives to experiment with information-revealing proposals early while preserving flexibility for better deals later. However, because each proposal can be accepted by either of the two responders and the realized partner is random when there are multiple acceptances, proposers must design trades that are acceptable regardless of which counterpart they match with, or else restrict themselves to deals likely to be accepted by exactly one responder.

Overall, the likely pattern is: Round 0 features exploratory but cautious trades, especially involving red for green or blue at relatively conservative ratios. As the game proceeds, Bear will attempt to accumulate blue while giving up some combination of red and green, and Buffalo and Goose will be willing to take small gains in green or convert their least-valued colors while avoiding large, clearly negative trades. Coordination problems, the random tie-breaking, and valuation uncertainty make some mutually beneficial trades fail, especially early, but at least a few modestly positive-sum trades are expected to go through over three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip: RED=10, BLUE=10, GREEN=10. No one yet knows the others’ red/blue values, so proposals are partly exploratory. Buffalo, with very low red value and modest blue value, is inclined to sell red or possibly blue in small quantities to learn others’ willingness to pay, while protecting green because it is a sure $0.50. Bear, who highly values blue, is looking for opportunities to buy blue cheaply using red or green, but must be careful not to offer too much green given it is common-value. Goose, nearly indifferent between red and blue and somewhat cautious with green, will tend to accept only clear gains, especially small green gains or additional chips that don’t involve large sacrifices. Round 0 proposals are likely to be conservative in size and fair-seeming in ratios, with at most one or two trades going through as players test the waters.

Holdings after Round 0: Buffalo now has RED=10, BLUE=7, GREEN=11 (having sold 3 BLUE to Bear for 2 GREEN); Bear has RED=10, BLUE=13, GREEN=8; Goose remains at RED=10, BLUE=10, GREEN=10. The first trade revealed that at least one counterpart (Buffalo) was willing to part with blue at 3-for-2 green, which for Bear was highly profitable, and for Buffalo a small but acceptable gain. Players now infer some differences in blue valuation: Bear’s strong demand is plausible, and Buffalo seems open to trading blue at modest markups. Round 1’s dynamics therefore shift toward more targeted attempts: Bear will keep trying to buy blue with green, but Buffalo may raise the “price” after seeing immediate acceptance last round. Goose has not yet traded and may either remain cautious or seek a first small gain. However, with time passing, players will look for slightly bolder yet still clearly beneficial deals, while staying wary of giving up too much green.

Holdings after Round 1: Buffalo has RED=10, BLUE=4, GREEN=13 (another trade of 3 BLUE for 2 GREEN, this time with Bear); Bear has RED=10, BLUE=16, GREEN=6; Goose still has RED=10, BLUE=10, GREEN=10. The pattern is now clear: Bear is systematically accumulating blue, paying with green at a 3:2 ratio, which is very favorable given Bear’s high blue value. Buffalo has gained green at a small private surplus each time, and has offloaded blue that is only moderately valuable to her. Goose has yet to trade, reflecting cautious behavior and a reluctance to give up green for low-value red/blue. In the final round, time pressure increases: it’s the last chance to re-balance portfolios. Buffalo now has relatively low blue and high green, but little incentive to reverse previous trades given her lower blue value. Bear might like additional blue, but must protect her limited green. Goose might seek a single modest gain, but also has reason to stay put rather than risk losses. As a result, players will either attempt one last, slightly aggressive but still seemingly fair trade or simply avoid trades that don’t offer clear gains, so only a small number of additional deals, if any, are expected to occur.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED the most ($1.00), GREEN next ($0.50 common), and BLUE the least (otter=$0.40, peacock=$0.50, cat=$0.70). The only disagreement in values is over BLUE, where cat values BLUE much more than the others; this creates the main scope for mutually beneficial trades. Players begin with symmetric endowments (10 of each color), so no one is forced to trade, and any accepted deal will typically involve giving up lower-valued BLUE for higher-valued RED or GREEN, or vice versa depending on perspective. However, because each player only knows their own valuations, they treat the public ranges for RED/BLUE as noisy signals rather than exact values. Otter is somewhat fairness-minded and cautious, so she will tend to propose clearly beneficial trades for herself but not extremely exploitative ones, and she will shy away from risky deals with low expected surplus. Peacock is relatively tolerant and unlikely to reject small but positive gains, so she will often accept modest improvements. Cat is both analytically strong and comfortable with low offers in ultimatum-like settings, so she is willing to take even very small positive gains, especially when they involve gaining BLUE chips that she privately values highly. Over three rounds, the most likely profitable pattern is that cat accumulates extra BLUE by selling away some RED/GREEN, while otter and peacock slowly swap away BLUE they don’t value as much in exchange for more RED/GREEN. Uncertainty about others’ values will keep early offers conservative; once someone sees others accepting favorable trades, they infer that counterparts probably don’t value BLUE as much and may try to push slightly harder terms in later rounds. Competition between peacock and cat for being the one chosen when multiple players accept creates an incentive to be slightly more generous or flexible as proposers, but since the game is short and each proposal is one-shot with random recipient selection, most players focus on straightforward, obviously profitable exchanges rather than intricate multi-step strategies.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any behavior yet, so they rely mainly on their own values and the public ranges. They know that RED and GREEN are likely valuable to everyone (GREEN is common-value), while BLUE is more uncertain but probably somewhat weaker for at least some players. Otter goes first and, being fairness-oriented but cautious, will likely test the waters with a mild, slightly self-favoring trade that doesn’t look outrageous. Peacock then moves, having seen otter’s attempt and any acceptance behavior, and is likely to make a moderate, value-improving offer that trades away her relatively less-valued BLUE or GREEN for more RED. Cat moves last and, being sharper and more comfortable with low ultimatum offers, is likely to construct an offer that takes advantage of her high private value for BLUE by selling RED or GREEN for BLUE in ways that still leave a small, safe gain for potential partners. Because everyone starts with plenty of chips, no one needs to take a loss; so all accepted trades should yield nonnegative surplus on both sides, with cat more willing than others to accept even small positive gains.

By the second round, players have seen that peacock managed to trade 3 BLUE for 2 RED with cat, revealing that at least one counterparty values BLUE enough to take that deal. Otter and peacock infer that someone (likely cat, given her later behavior) might have a relatively high BLUE value, which makes future requests for BLUE more plausible. Cat, having just gained BLUE and given up some RED, now holds an even more BLUE-heavy portfolio and cares about rebuilding RED and GREEN only if she can do so at favorable terms. Otter again opens the round and, after seeing her first offer rejected and peacock’s deal succeed, shifts toward trading away BLUE (which she values moderately) to get more RED. Peacock’s mid-round offer is informed by the fact that cat accepted a previous BLUE-for-RED trade; she will likely propose a slightly more favorable deal for herself but not so aggressive that it risks universal rejection. Cat’s last move in this round reflects that she can exploit her high BLUE value to accept low surplus on some trades and will search for trades that convert some BLUE into additional RED or maintain her surplus while making it slightly harder for others to profit from BLUE.

In the final round, everyone has seen a consistent pattern: cat is willing to take BLUE in exchange for RED at rates that give her a small positive gain, while otter and peacock each twice declined to buy BLUE for GREEN. This suggests a rough shared belief that GREEN is about as valuable as BLUE for otter and peacock, but that cat especially values BLUE. As a result, offers in the last round are more targeted. Peacock now opens, likely aiming one more time to trade away her BLUE to cat for RED at slightly improved terms. Otter follows, remembering that cat is often an eager taker of BLUE-for-RED deals; she constructs a final offer that is safe and more favorable than her earlier attempt but not so skewed that it risks rejection. Cat, seeing her BLUE-heavy position and minimal remaining time, will either try a last attempt to trade surplus BLUE for RED or GREEN at acceptable terms, or accept that her previous successful trades already captured much of the low-hanging surplus. Risk aversion and short horizon make all three players avoid any trade that would create even a small expected loss, and they rely heavily on the on-screen surplus calculator to verify that each proposed or received deal is at least weakly beneficial given their private valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features symmetric initial endowments (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN each) but asymmetric private values on RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so surplus comes mainly from reallocating RED and BLUE to players who value them more. badger values both RED and BLUE highly ($0.80 each), camel moderately prefers RED ($0.60) over BLUE ($0.40), and squid prefers BLUE ($0.60) over cheap RED ($0.20). Privately, each participant sees their own valuations and a surplus calculator for any considered or observed trade, but does not see others’ values. They must infer who likes which chips from observed proposals and responses over just three short rounds.

The core efficiency pattern is: RED should flow from squid (who values it least) to badger and camel, and BLUE should flow from camel (who values it least) toward badger and squid. GREEN is neutral at $0.50 for everyone and may serve as a balancing chip or remain unused. Because each player only knows their own values, early proposals likely reveal tentative guesses: simple, small, or symmetric trades that appear plausibly fair. Personas matter for acceptance behavior: camel is very tolerant of small gains and unlikely to reject positive-surplus deals; squid is cooperative and somewhat generous but still sensitive to value; badger is moderately fair but also somewhat selective about trades.

Strategically, proposers want to craft offers that are clearly beneficial to themselves (as shown by their calculator) but also likely to give recipients at least a small gain, given unknown valuations. Offers that are too one-sided risk rejections from the more fairness-sensitive players, but overtly generous trades sacrifice value. Simultaneous responses plus random selection among multiple accepters create a mild coordination problem: if more than one responder sees a gain, they all may accept, and the proposer cannot choose the partner. Over three rounds, players can adjust based on observed acceptances and rejections, but there is limited time to fully learn the others’ preferences. A plausible trajectory is conservative, low-stakes trades in Round 0 that start to move RED from squid to camel/badger and BLUE from camel to squid/badger; modestly more targeted and confident trades in Round 1 once patterns emerge; and final, opportunistic or clean-up trades in Round 2, focusing on any remaining obvious arbitrage while avoiding complex or risky proposals.

rounds.round_explanation: In Round 0, all players start with symmetric holdings and no direct evidence about others’ chip values. They see only the public value ranges and know their own valuations via the calculator. Proposers are likely to begin with simple, relatively small trades that seem plausibly acceptable to either potential partner. badger, knowing RED and BLUE are both valuable, will be cautious about giving them away cheaply and may initially use GREEN as a neutral medium. camel, with strong numeracy and high tolerance for small gains, is willing to accept modestly profitable trades even if they appear only slightly beneficial. squid, who values BLUE much more than RED and is highly cognitively sophisticated, is inclined to accept any clear positive-surplus BLUE-for-RED deals and to infer that others probably also have stronger preferences for one color over the other. This round is about exploration and signaling: identifying who is willing to trade what without incurring big losses.

By Round 1, one successful trade (camel exchanging RED for squid’s BLUE) has slightly tilted holdings: camel now holds more BLUE and less RED, while squid holds more RED and less BLUE. This executed trade suggests that both camel and squid see some gain in swapping RED for BLUE at roughly one-to-one quantities, hinting that camel values RED more than BLUE and squid values BLUE more than RED. badger, having had their initial GREEN-for-RED probe rejected, may infer that at least one opponent is reluctant to part with GREEN for RED at that rate and may shift toward directly targeting BLUE–RED swaps where surplus is more obvious. Proposers can now exploit emerging patterns: squid is a likely taker of BLUE-for-RED deals, and camel seems tolerant of marginal trades. However, each player still lacks precise knowledge of exact valuations, so offers remain modest and not fully optimized.

Entering Round 2, trade history has revealed a clear pattern: camel repeatedly trades away BLUE to gain RED, squid repeatedly trades away RED to gain BLUE, and badger is willing to move RED and BLUE in ways that yield them moderate personal gains. Everyone now has a better qualitative sense: camel is the RED-preferrer, squid is the BLUE-preferrer, and badger values both fairly highly and can arbitrage between them. With only one final proposal turn each, players focus on simple, clearly positive deals that adjust marginal imbalances without risking rejections or overcomplicated trades. Because green chips have remained largely unused and offer no comparative advantage to anyone, final trades concentrate on small RED–BLUE swaps that fine-tune portfolios: squid and camel each seek a bit more of their favored color, while badger looks for safe, positive-surplus opportunities that exploit the known directional preferences.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known red/blue values and common-value green chips. Dog values red highly (1.00) and blue weakly (0.20); butterfly values blue highly (0.90) and red moderately (0.60); ram values both red (0.40) and blue (0.10) low. All value greens at 0.50. This creates strong gains-from-trade: dog is the natural red seller and blue buyer; butterfly is the natural blue seller and moderate red buyer; ram is indifferent-ish to red/blue and may trade when terms are favorable but is unlikely to drive big deals. Uncertainty comes from each player only seeing their own values; they know that others’ red/blue values lie within 0.10–1.00 but not the exact mapping. Because acceptance decisions reveal exact personal surplus via the calculator, responders will almost never accept strictly negative-surplus trades. Still, they may accept small positive-surplus trades even if the surplus is modest, especially given ultimatum priors suggesting relatively low rejection thresholds. Dog, with high empathy and cooperation, is likely to make somewhat generous proposals that still exploit her comparative advantage in red; butterfly, being numerate and valuing blue highly, will seek to trade blue away at good markups while acquiring red cheaply; ram will aim mainly for small, safe gains. Strategic uncertainty arises because each proposer must balance making offers attractive enough to some unknown-value partner while not giving up too much surplus, and must anticipate that if both responders accept, the partner is chosen at random. This lowers the incentive for discriminatory proposals and pushes senders toward offers that are at least weakly beneficial to both responders. Over three rounds, we should see early, discovery-oriented offers in round 0, followed by more targeted trades once players infer who seems eager to move which colors. By round 2, the largest easy gains should be mostly realized, and surviving proposals will be more marginal or fine-tuning inventory. Overall, the most likely realized big trades are dog selling red to butterfly in exchange for blue or green, and butterfly selling blue to dog in exchange for green or red, with ram occasionally capturing small, essentially risk-free improvements when favorable bundles appear.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one yet knows the others’ specific values, only that red and blue are privately valued in the 0.10–1.00 range and green is always 0.50. Players will likely probe the market with simple one-color-for-one-color trades that appear roughly fair at face value. Dog, valuing red most, is reluctant to give up red cheaply but may initially test small red-for-blue or red-for-green trades. Butterfly, valuing blue highly, will try to siphon blue value out of others by offering red/green. Ram, less extreme in valuations and highly uncertainty-averse, will tend to propose conservative, nearly value-neutral trades that are likely to be accepted and reveal others’ willingness to move chips. Because this is the first round, responders will also use acceptance decisions to learn who seems desperate to move which colors.

By round 1, dog and butterfly have already interacted once, and ram has executed a small trade with dog. These early trades reveal partial information: dog is willing to give up some red for blue at what appears to butterfly as a favorable rate, hinting that dog values red especially highly or blue especially low; ram’s willingness to exchange blue for green at a ratio slightly worse than break-even for ram suggests strong risk aversion and a modest valuation of blue. Now holdings are slightly imbalanced: dog holds extra green and is somewhat down in red; butterfly has more red and fewer blue; ram has fewer green and more blue than at start. Players will start to refine their strategies: butterfly becomes more confident that buying red from dog is profitable but must craft terms dog will accept; dog will look for ways to rebuild red or offload low-value blue; ram, having gained a small safe surplus, may be even more selective, waiting for clearly positive deals. Proposals will tend to be more targeted based on this inferred structure, seeking larger gains while still avoiding obvious rejections.

Entering round 2, several patterns are clearer: dog is a consistent net seller of red only when getting strong compensation in blue/green and is the most enthusiastic buyer of green from ram; butterfly profits from both red and green but is especially interested in holding blue, making him cautious about parting with it; ram has demonstrated a baseline willingness to accept small positive-surplus trades, especially for green, but is not chasing aggressive deals. Inventories now reflect earlier trades: dog is relatively green- and blue-heavy but red-light; butterfly has more red and somewhat fewer greens; ram has replenished some greens and moderately altered red/blue balances. With only three turns left, players focus on final, relatively safe surplus extractions. Dog may still try to reacquire red or shed low-value blue if she can get green; butterfly will look for a last high-margin blue-for-red swap but must recognize others’ resistance; ram is most likely to accept clearly favorable, small-scale trades and reject any that risk losses. We expect at most one more accepted trade, probably modest in size, as the largest arbitrage opportunities have already been exploited in prior rounds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips, with common-value green chips at $0.50 for everyone. All players start with 10 of each color, so initial holdings are symmetric but underlying payoffs are not: alligator values BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.30; lion values BLUE=$1.00, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.40; iguana values BLUE=$0.40, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.80. Overall, lion is the strongest buyer of blue and weakest holder of red relative to others, while iguana is the strongest buyer of red and weakest holder of blue, and alligator sits in between, preferring blue over red but less extremely. Because green is common-value and sits between their red and blue valuations, it mainly acts as a neutral store of value. The key tension is that players only see their own private values; they must infer the others’ preferences from observed trades. The equilibrium-like pressure is toward moving chips from low-value to high-value holders (e.g., red from alligator and lion toward iguana; blue from iguana toward lion), creating scope for mutually beneficial trades. But uncertainty about what others value most, plus the random partner selection when multiple players accept, makes players cautious about proposing deals that look too generous or risk giving away surplus. Given the ultimatum-task priors, none of these players are highly punitive or rejection-prone: they are inclined to accept even modestly positive deals, particularly iguana, who is extremely tolerant of low shares. Over only three rounds, each with one proposal per player, the likely dynamics are: (1) early feeling-out proposals where players test whether others will trade at modest but safe surpluses, (2) mid-game adjustment as they infer at least partial rank-order of others’ preferences from what is accepted, and (3) late-game attempts to capture remaining arbitrage in their favor, if they see profitable opportunities, constrained by limited time and existing holdings. Because proposals and acceptances are purely self-interested and informed by on-screen surplus calculators, accepted trades will almost always improve value for both sides, but they won’t fully exhaust all possible gains from trade within such a short horizon.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color and know only their own values. Alligator knows she values blue more than red and will look to trade away red for blue at a profit, but she is unsure if the others see blue or red as more valuable. Lion strongly prefers blue and mildly prefers red over green, so she will be keen to acquire blue if possible, or red from low-red-valuers, while offloading some green. Iguana strongly prefers red over blue and values green in between, so she will be inclined to trade away some blue for red or possibly for green if profitable. Because this is the first round, players are likely to start with conservative, near risk-free positive-surplus trades for themselves, even if it means giving only modest surplus to others. The big uncertainty is which counterpart (lion or iguana) will accept a given proposal, since acceptances are simultaneous and there is randomization if both agree.

After round 0, one trade has executed: alligator traded 4 RED to lion for 3 BLUE. Current holdings are: alligator (RED=6, BLUE=13, GREEN=10), lion (RED=14, BLUE=7, GREEN=10), iguana (RED=10, BLUE=10, GREEN=10). The executed trade reveals partial information: both alligator and lion signaled a willingness to move red against blue at fairly symmetric terms, suggesting to each that the other values blue at least somewhat more than red. Lion’s declined green-for-red offer and iguana’s declined blue-for-red proposal suggest that counterparties are not yet comfortable with more aggressive swaps that heavily favor one side in private valuations. Given these signals, players adjust: alligator now has a large blue stack and may cautiously try to convert some blue or green into more valuable configurations if she can exploit differences, but she also wants to avoid undoing her earlier gains. Lion now holds a lot of red and may want to offload some low-marginal-value red for blue or green, while being wary that others might undervalue red. Iguana has inferred that others do not snap-accept red-for-green or red-for-blue trades that look lopsided to her; she may try proposals that share surplus more visibly, hoping to get at least small positive gains accepted before the game ends.

Before round 2, executed trades in round 1 have further reshaped holdings and revealed more preferences. After alligator’s RED-for-GREEN swap with lion and lion’s RED-for-BLUE swap with alligator, holdings are: alligator (RED=7, BLUE=15, GREEN=8), lion (RED=11, BLUE=5, GREEN=12), iguana (RED=10, BLUE=10, GREEN=10). These accepted trades show that lion is willing to give up some green and red for more blue, consistent with a strong blue preference, and that alligator still sees red as her weakest asset and is comfortable trading it away for either green or blue at modestly favorable rates. Iguana’s simple RED-for-GREEN proposal was still declined, signaling either misaligned terms or others’ caution about late trades that slightly favor iguana’s private values. With only one round left, players may try to lock in remaining safe surplus opportunities but will likely avoid any proposal that risks obvious loss, given the surplus calculators. Alligator, now very blue-heavy, will be hesitant to trade away her best-valued color unless she gets a large concession. Lion might want more blue but lacks attractive chips to offer without creating losses for others. Iguana still wants red, but given repeated declines and limited time, she may either stop proposing or offer very mild improvements that others still see as not worth executing this late in the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game features private values over red and blue chips and a common value for green. All start with 10 of each color. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, so trading green is zero-sum: no one can generate surplus by exchanging green for any other green, and any green-for-green trade is pointless. Surplus instead comes from reallocating red and blue chips between players whose valuations differ. Whale and Duck both value red at $1.00, while Dolphin values red at $0.60, so Dolphin is the natural seller of red and Whale/Duck are natural buyers. For blue, Dolphin values them most ($0.80), Duck in the middle ($0.60), and Whale least ($0.40), so Whale is a natural seller of blue and Dolphin is the main buyer; Duck sits in between and can act as an intermediary in some trades. Because each player only knows their own valuations, their initial beliefs about others’ preferences must be inferred from the public range and observed behavior over the three rounds.

The UI gives each player exact net gain or loss from any posted trade given their own private values, so acceptance decisions are likely driven mainly by whether the trade is strictly profitable and secondarily by fairness, risk, and expectations about future opportunities. Ultimatum profiles suggest all three are tolerant of low offers and rarely reject positive gains, especially Dolphin and Duck, who accepted even minimal splits previously. Whale also tends to accept low but is somewhat more assertive in prior offers. With three rounds and a fixed proposer order, each player has three chances to propose and many chances to accept. They don’t observe others’ valuations directly, but as accepted trades reveal patterns (e.g., a player eagerly buys red or blue at certain terms), they can update beliefs.

Strategically, Whale will likely try early trades that exchange her low-value blue for either red or green, testing which counterpart is more willing to sell. Dolphin has strong demand for blue and weaker demand for red; he can profit by buying blue from Whale (cheap to her, valuable to him) and selling red to Whale or Duck if he later infers their red is highly valued. Duck has high value on red and moderate value on blue; she will be interested in trades where she gives away some blue or possibly green to obtain red, and she may also accept trades that give her more blue if the price in red or green is modest.

Uncertainty and three-player competition introduce complications: when a trade would be profitable for more than one responder, multiple acceptance is possible, and a random recipient is chosen. That dilutes the expected value of proposing "too attractive" deals. Proposers must balance making offers generous enough to ensure at least one acceptance against keeping them narrow enough that not everyone wants to jump in. Over three rounds, players may shift from exploratory trades—probing others’ tastes—to more targeted surplus-extracting proposals once some patterns are revealed. Since the horizon is short and all are relatively acceptance-prone, the game is likely to see several executed trades that move chips from low- to high-value holders, especially reallocating red from Dolphin to Whale/Duck and blue from Whale/Duck to Dolphin, with green serving mostly as a low-risk, focal medium in early tests or as a tie-breaker when valuations for red and blue are closer.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, everyone starts with symmetric holdings (10 of each color) and only their own valuations. Offers are likely exploratory: players test whether others treat a color as cheap or expensive. Given the public range that red and blue can be anywhere between $0.10 and $1.00 and that green is a certain $0.50, early proposals probably use green as a safe, common anchor and try small, mildly asymmetric ratios that are profitable for the proposer but still likely positive for at least one responder. All three players have low ultimatum rejection tendencies, so modest but strictly profitable deals should be accepted. The public history after this round will begin to reveal which players favor which colors.

By the start of round 1, the only executed trade shows Dolphin willing to accept a slight loss in green for a modest gain in blue, and Whale willing to part with some blue, signaling that Whale likely values blue relatively less than green while Dolphin values blue more. Failed proposals involving symmetric green-for-blue and green-for-red swaps suggest neither Whale nor Duck sees strong benefit in exchanging green at 1:1 for other colors absent stronger evidence of advantage. Players now suspect Dolphin has relatively high blue value and moderate red value, while Whale may have especially cheap blue. Duck has not revealed much yet beyond declining a neutral green trade for red, hinting that she values red at least as much as green. With limited information and two rounds left, proposers may attempt slightly more tailored offers: Whale can try to sell more blue to Dolphin at more favorable ratios, and Dolphin and Duck may start to experiment with red-for-blue terms as they infer preferences.

At the start of the final round, the history shows a clear pattern: Dolphin repeatedly accepts deals that yield her blue, even at the cost of green or red, revealing high blue valuation. Whale has repeatedly agreed to trades that shed blue in exchange for green or blue in exchange for red, consistent with blue being relatively low-value and red high-value for her. Duck has now executed one trade buying red with blue from Dolphin, consistent with Duck also valuing red strongly. With only one proposal each remaining, players focus on consolidating advantageous positions: Whale is likely to push once more to offload blue for either red or green, Dolphin may try to pivot some of her accumulated red into additional blue or green if profitable, and Duck may seek a final chance to convert surplus blue or green into red. Because it is the last round, concerns about revealing preferences for future bargaining vanish; decisions center on immediate net gain, though three-player competition still tempers overly generous proposals that might attract multiple acceptors.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game has clear efficiency gains from moving chips toward the players who value them most, but players only know their own values. All three start with the same 10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN, and GREEN is common-value at $0.50 each. The key private differences are: turtle values BLUE highest ($1.00) and RED at $0.90; gorilla values RED highest ($1.00) and BLUE at $0.70; zebra values both RED and BLUE relatively low ($0.60 and $0.50). In terms of pure surplus, RED should migrate to gorilla (highest RED value), BLUE should migrate to turtle (highest BLUE value), and zebra is a natural net seller of both RED and BLUE to the others while holding indifferent GREEN. But no one sees others’ payoffs, so they infer from observed behavior and the public chip ranges. The UI surplus calculator makes each accept/decline decision myopically rational with respect to their own values: nobody will knowingly accept a negative-surplus trade. However, they can still post weak or unbalanced proposals because they don’t see others’ values. Personality priors suggest: turtle is moderately fair and somewhat cautious, likely to propose mutually beneficial trades but not extreme concessions; gorilla is highly cooperative and tolerant of small gains, likely to accept slightly lopsided but still-positive trades; zebra is very generous as a proposer but has a medium rejection rate, so as a responder she won’t take clear losses, but she is willing to accept modest gains and to help deals happen. Over three rounds, with everyone proposing once per round, we expect some experimentation early, followed by more targeted trades once at least one beneficial pattern is discovered. The main efficient patterns that can realistically emerge in such a short game are turtle buying BLUE from zebra (or gorilla) and gorilla buying RED from zebra (or turtle), with GREEN sometimes used as a medium of exchange because it’s commonly valued. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, senders face uncertainty about their actual counterpart if both non-senders find a proposal attractive; this slightly discourages proposals that are very good for both potential recipients, but given the small number of rounds, players are more likely to just aim for clearly positive trades and accept the lottery when it arises.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one has any information about others’ private values except the public ranges and the common GREEN value. Strategic uncertainty is highest here. Proposers are likely to start with simple, easily evaluated offers involving GREEN as a safe currency because everyone knows it is worth $0.50 to all. Turtle, valuing BLUE at $1 and RED at $0.90, has strong incentives to acquire BLUE and is willing to part with some RED or GREEN. Gorilla wants RED and can pay with BLUE or GREEN. Zebra has the weakest valuations of RED and BLUE and so is a natural supplier but doesn’t yet know that. Early in this round, proposals will likely be somewhat conservative and exploratory, focusing on obvious private gains without presuming others’ valuations. Rejections do not reveal exact values but will gradually inform each proposer about which price ratios others will not accept.

By the start of round 1, each player has observed three accepted trades and can infer some loose constraints: zebra was willing to trade BLUE away for GREEN at roughly even terms (since she accepted turtle’s 2 GREEN for 2 BLUE) and to trade BLUE for RED with gorilla, and gorilla accepted a trade where she gave BLUE for RED and later gave RED for GREEN. This suggests to attentive players that gorilla values RED at least as much as BLUE and GREEN, and zebra is relatively indifferent across these colors inside the allowed ranges, or at least willing to make small positive trades. Turtle observes that others are accepting offers that imply they do not have extremely skewed valuations. Strategically, this is the first round in which proposers can start to refine offers toward the directions consistent with efficiency: turtle trying more aggressively to buy BLUE, gorilla to buy RED, and zebra serving as a flexible middle trader, often using GREEN as a balancing chip. However, with only a few data points and no numerical values from others, proposals still remain relatively simple and near-even in chip quantities, while tilted just enough to give the sender a clear private surplus.

Going into round 2, the final round, everyone has seen a consistent pattern: zebra repeatedly trades away BLUE and often RED in exchange for GREEN or whatever others offer at close to one-for-one rates, and gorilla reliably accepts trades that give her more RED or slightly more total value, even at modest gains. Turtle has already secured extra BLUE and offloaded some RED, and gorilla has accumulated RED while parting with some BLUE and GREEN; zebra has been the main liquidity provider, moving toward a more GREEN-heavy portfolio. With only three turns left, each proposer now faces a last-chance problem: push for clearly beneficial trades while avoiding offers that are so one-sided they get rejected. Because all still face uncertainty about exact values, they continue using simple ratios—mostly 1:1 or close quantities—but now targeted: turtle will try to further tilt her portfolio toward BLUE, gorilla will try to top up RED if possible or recover some GREEN at no loss, and zebra, who appears comfortable making small positive trades, may offer another balancing exchange that yields a slight gain in her own calculator while facilitating others’ objectives.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 each for everyone) and privately-valued red and blue chips. All start with 10 of each color. Red is relatively valuable to cat ($0.90) and iguana ($0.80) but low for zebra ($0.40). Blue is most valuable to zebra ($1.00), moderately valuable to cat ($0.60), and least to iguana ($0.30). Because green is fixed at $0.50 for all, it mainly serves as a medium of exchange or a safe store of value. The core efficiency gains come from moving red chips away from zebra toward cat or iguana, and moving blue chips away from iguana toward zebra or cat. However, each player only knows their own values, so early in the game there is substantial uncertainty about how others rank red vs. blue. Players can infer some preferences from which colors are offered vs. requested. The ultimatum profiles suggest relatively tolerant responders with low rejection thresholds, so they are inclined to accept any strictly positive gain. In a three-player setting, senders must also account for the random partner selection if multiple others accept; they cannot hand-pick the more profitable partner. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), players are likely to start with modest, low-risk, small-quantity trades that look at least weakly appealing to more than one counterparty, then gradually sharpen their targeting as they infer others’ preferences from accept/decline patterns. Because there is no future penalty for tough bargaining beyond lost opportunities in this one-shot game, proposers will often aim for trades that give themselves a clear positive surplus while offering small but positive gains to at least one potential recipient, testing the waters rather than revealing their full valuations. The resulting history is likely to show early feeler offers with mixed acceptance, followed by more directed red-for-blue or red-for-green exchanges once relative values become clearer.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, no one has observed any trades, so beliefs about others’ chip values are broad. All players know greens are common-value at $0.50, but they must infer others’ red/blue valuations from offers. Cat and iguana are numerate and cautious but with low ultimatum rejection thresholds; zebra is more heuristic but also very tolerant of small gains. In this first round, we expect small, conservative offers that test whether others are willing to give up a color cheaply. Proposers are unlikely to make large, risky trades before seeing at least one acceptance pattern.

After round 0 trades, players have some initial signals. Cat has revealed some willingness to give up green for blue and to accept blue-for-red from iguana, which hints that red is valuable but that cat still likes blue relative to green. Iguana has indicated a desire to accumulate red at the cost of blue, showing red > blue for her. Zebra tried to dump red for green but found no takers, a weak signal that others are not eager to pay much for red at that price. Now beliefs narrow: everyone suspects red is important for at least one counterparty and that iguana is relatively blue-poor in valuation. Round 1 should feature more targeted offers, especially focused on red-for-blue or red-for-green, but still with moderate quantities as players continue to learn who values what.

By round 2, trade history reveals clear patterns: iguana consistently trades away blue for red or other colors, signaling blue is relatively weak for her. Zebra has again tried to offload red, indicating that red is her least-valued color and that she is willing to pay with it for either green or blue. Cat has repeatedly accepted deals where she gains red even when paying with blue or green, making it clear that red is strongly valued. With only one round left, players shift from exploratory trades to attempts to lock in final portfolio improvements: cat and iguana both prefer more red, zebra prefers more blue, and greens serve as a neutral fallback. Proposers are now willing to make somewhat more aggressive yet still positive-sum trades, knowing there is no future round in which to exploit hidden information.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features symmetric endowments (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN for each player) but asymmetric, privately known values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value and neutral ($0.50 to everyone) so it can serve as a flexible medium of exchange or a baseline asset. All three know only their own valuations but can infer others’ preferences via revealed behavior over the three rounds. Gorilla values both RED and BLUE relatively low and symmetrically ($0.30 each), so she is the least attached to either colored chip and is generally willing to trade if she gets a small gain on her calculator. Camel values both RED and BLUE more than Gorilla, particularly BLUE ($0.80) and RED ($0.50), so he benefits from acquiring either color at the expense of GREEN or from less valuable chips. Monkey is the most BLUE-loving ($0.90) but values RED very low ($0.20); this makes him especially eager to dump RED for BLUE or GREEN and generally reluctant to part with BLUE unless he obtains a large RED or GREEN compensation. The strategic tension comes from the rule that offers must be one color for another, and acceptance is simultaneous and non-discretionary: if both non-senders accept, the actual partner is chosen randomly, so proposers cannot hand-pick partners. With three short rounds, players cannot fully learn everyone’s values but can still update beliefs as they see who eagerly accepts which trades. Player personas matter mainly via acceptance thresholds and willingness to propose thin-margin offers: Gorilla is relatively cooperative and tolerant, accepting small positive gains; Camel is hard-bargaining as proposer (low fairness offer in ultimatum) but still accepts small positive gains; Monkey has somewhat stricter thresholds and is more willing to reject offers that yield only tiny or ambiguous gains. Across the nine proposal turns, we should expect: (1) early, relatively exploratory trades where Gorilla and Camel offer simple RED–BLUE or GREEN–BLUE swaps that yield clear surplus to at least one side; (2) Monkey focusing on unloading RED and accumulating BLUE, occasionally using GREEN when that still yields him clear dollar gains; (3) sparsely accepted trades because each proposal must give a positive calculator gain to the responder, and any slight miscalibration will be declined by the stricter Monkey; and (4) overall movement in the direction of Camel and Monkey holding more of their favored BLUE, while Gorilla ends up with more GREEN and a somewhat balanced mix of RED/BLUE that reflects her more indifferent valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, chip holdings are symmetric and no one has observed others’ behavior yet. Gorilla moves first and, being relatively cooperative with low rejection thresholds, is likely to propose a modest, slightly self-favoring trade using GREEN as a sweetener to attract at least one partner. Camel, analytically strong and hard-bargaining, will likely design a proposal that gives him a clear surplus in his calculator by acquiring BLUE or RED without offering too many GREEN. Monkey, aware of his strong preference for BLUE over RED, will probably make an early attempt to convert RED into BLUE in a way that seems obviously profitable to at least one partner. Given the lack of information, offers in this first round may be somewhat exploratory but still rational: they are set so that the proposer sees a clear positive gain on the surplus calculator, while guessing that at least one other player might also see a positive gain.

By the start of Round 1, Gorilla has already traded GREEN for BLUE with Camel and then traded some BLUE for RED with Monkey. Camel has profited from the first trade by acquiring underpriced BLUE in exchange for GREEN, but failed to complete his own slightly aggressive RED-for-BLUE proposal in Round 0. Monkey has successfully dumped some RED for BLUE via Gorilla and seen Camel decline a RED-for-BLUE deal that would have been favorable to Monkey; this hints to Monkey that Camel values BLUE highly and is cautious about parting with it. Now, Gorilla has learned that both partners like BLUE enough to consider selling RED or GREEN for it, while Monkey perceives that Gorilla is relatively flexible and not too demanding. In this round, Gorilla is likely to continue making modest, calculator-positive swaps that slightly rebalance her portfolio toward GREEN and away from her low-value BLUE or RED. Camel, having seen his prior offer declined, may shade his proposals slightly more generous while still preserving a decent surplus for himself, often using GREEN as currency to secure preferred chips. Monkey, now more aware of valuations indirectly, will likely keep trying to move RED out and accumulate BLUE or, secondarily, GREEN, but may start targeting Camel when he suspects Camel’s willingness to pay for BLUE with GREEN or RED is limited.

Going into the final round, some structure is clear from revealed behavior. Gorilla has repeatedly been willing to part with BLUE for GREEN or RED when the calculator shows even modest gains, confirming to others that she values BLUE relatively low and is happy with small positive deals. Camel has shown that he is eager to acquire BLUE but also cautious about giving too much RED or GREEN in return, refusing trades that leave him with small or unclear gains. Monkey has consistently tried to offload RED for BLUE and has accepted trades that provide strong BLUE gains, revealing his high BLUE and low RED valuation. This last round is short and players know there is no future beyond it, so they have no incentive to leave surplus on the table but also no reason to invest in reputation. As a result, proposals are either clearly beneficial to the proposer and at least one partner or they are simply declined. Camel, starting the round, will likely design a final attempt to grab more BLUE using his still-valuable GREEN, trying to ensure at least one of Gorilla or Monkey gets a noticeable gain. Gorilla, moving second, is likely to pursue one more modest value-improving swap that rebalances away from her inferior chips. Monkey, with the last move of the game, will attempt a final trade to convert remaining RED into BLUE or GREEN, but faced with increasingly lopsided portfolios and more sharply perceived valuations, the last trade is at higher risk of being declined if it does not offer a clear mutual gain.
