parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip trading game creates strong gains-from-trade between red and blue chips because players value them very differently, while green chips are a constant $0.50 to everyone and serve as a natural medium of exchange. Dolphin highly values blue (1.00) and dislikes red (0.20), frog highly values red (1.00) and dislikes blue (0.30), and pig moderately values both red and blue (0.40 each). Each starts with 10 of each color. Because players only know their own values, they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. The surplus calculator makes it easy to see one’s own gains or losses from any proposed trade, so rejections mostly occur when a trade is either negative or only marginally positive for the responder compared with potential future opportunities or fairness concerns. Dolphin is cautious and somewhat fairness-oriented, so is unlikely to propose or accept deals that leave them clearly worse off, and may avoid extreme exploitation even when possible. Frog is very cooperative and tolerant as a responder, so will often accept small positive gains and may propose relatively generous terms. Pig is willing to accept nearly any non-negative offer and is less strict on fairness, so will be an eager trading partner if given any surplus. Over three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, the most likely dynamics are: (1) early exploration with small, safe trades that reveal value patterns (e.g., dolphin trying to swap red for blue or green, frog trying to acquire red, pig opportunistically accepting), (2) quick recognition that frog wants red and dolphin wants blue, with pig playing a flexible middle role, and (3) incremental rebalancing of inventories where responders accept whenever the calculator shows clear gains and perceived downside risk from future waiting is low. Uncertainty remains about how aggressively proposers push surplus to themselves versus sharing it, especially because proposers cannot target a specific responder when multiple accept; this encourages offers that are acceptable to at least one other player but not necessarily perfectly tailored to any single opponent.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one yet knows others’ private values, so proposals are likely to be exploratory but still self-serving. Dolphin, being risk-averse and numerate, will probably test a modest trade that clearly improves her own position without being obviously predatory. Frog, highly cooperative and very tolerant of low payoffs, is likely to post relatively generous offers to induce at least one partner to trade and begin revealing who values red or blue more. Pig, being highly accepting in ultimatum tasks and less reflective, will mainly look for any calculator-indicated gain and may post somewhat coarse, surplus-seeking proposals without precise targeting. Because responders move simultaneously and the partner is chosen at random if both accept, proposers will design offers that are attractive to at least one counterpart but still positive for themselves.

By the start of round 1, some structure is visible: dolphin has traded away low-value red for high-value blue, revealing a preference for blue and aversion to red to attentive observers; frog has obtained red from pig at roughly even terms, signaling that red might be valuable to frog; pig has been the main early trading partner and may appear flexible. Now proposers can refine their strategies: dolphin should continue to dump red for either blue or green, but must respect that frog and pig have inferred something about her preferences. Frog, realizing that red is attractive to her and that pig will accept modest gains, may offer pig small but positive improvements to keep funneling red away from dolphin. Pig, having seen both counterparts trade profitably with him, may now attempt more self-serving deals, though he remains likely to accept almost any non-negative response opportunity.

In the final round, everyone knows the game is ending, so there is no value in holding back inventory for better future trades. Each proposer should focus on any remaining trades that yield immediate positive surplus for both sides. Dolphin, who now has relatively few red chips left and many blue chips, may try to convert any remaining red into blue or green but will not sacrifice much value to do so. Frog, holding a sizable red position and fewer blue/green chips, may have limited scope for further profitable trade except possibly small exchanges with pig if pig still holds blue that frog does not especially value. Pig, being tolerant and opportunistic, is willing to take any small gain offered, especially if it involves swapping chip colors he values similarly. Because responder behavior is now more myopic and surplus-focused, offers that show even modest positive calculator gains are very likely to be accepted; extremely skewed or negative offers are likely to be rejected, especially by dolphin, who is more cautious.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features private values on red and blue chips with a common value for green chips. All players start with 10 of each color, and each chip can only change hands through bilateral trades initiated by the scheduled proposer. Everyone sees inventories and trade history, but no one knows others’ dollar values for red and blue, so there is strategic uncertainty about which colors are most valuable to whom and how aggressively others will extract surplus.

Given the values, red is especially valuable to owl-green-168 (RED $0.70) and relatively low value to the others (iguana $0.20, squid $0.10). Blue is particularly valuable to iguana-blue-1059 ($0.80), moderately valuable to squid ($0.50) and low to owl ($0.30). Green is common-value $0.50 and hence a natural "currency" that seems safe to trade because it has equal value to everyone and clarifies that no one is being exploited via information asymmetry there.

Iguana has high numeracy and is cooperative, so is likely to search for positive-sum trades that look reasonably fair, especially by using green and his low-value red to obtain more blue. Owl is comfortable accepting very small personal gains and rarely rejects in ultimatum-like settings; given her strong preference for red over blue and equal value for green, she is likely to sell blue or green cheaply to build red, and to accept many offers that give her even a slight gain. Squid is moderately cooperative, values blue more than red but less than iguana does, and is somewhat willing to accept modest gains; she will often accept small but positive changes, especially if they don’t risk making her look too exploited.

Because responders see their exact dollar gain/loss from each offer, we expect few strictly negative trades; the main frictions are that the proposer must make the same offer to both potential recipients and cannot target the higher-gain partner, and that proposers are uncertain how others rank red vs. blue. Trades that are highly favorable to multiple parties risk both recipients accepting and causing random partner selection, which is fine if the proposer’s gain is similar across recipients but risky if not. Over three rounds, we expect: (1) early exploratory offers using green to signal willingness to trade and to infer others’ color preferences; (2) owl gradually accumulating red by giving up blue or green; (3) iguana steadily trying to convert red (and sometimes green) into blue; and (4) squid acting as a flexible middle trader, accepting small positive-sum deals involving blue and green, and occasionally red if the price is right. The forecast below simulates one plausible path where most executed trades are modest but positive-sum for both sides, clustered around the owl–squid and iguana–squid relationships, with iguana struggling a bit more to secure trades due to being more fairness-minded and less willing to push strongly one-sided deals.

All valuations (per chip):
- iguana-blue-1059: RED $0.20, BLUE $0.80, GREEN $0.50
- owl-green-168: RED $0.70, BLUE $0.30, GREEN $0.50
- squid-purple-9957: RED $0.10, BLUE $0.50, GREEN $0.50.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, inventories are symmetric (10 of each color) and no one yet knows others’ private red/blue values. Round 0 is likely to feature exploratory offers. Iguana, who values blue highly and red weakly, will probably test a small trade to acquire blue or green using red. Owl, who strongly prefers red, will likely try to acquire red by giving up blue or green, and will be willing to accept modest gains. Squid, with intermediate valuations and high empathy, is poised to accept relatively small but positive trades and can quickly become a central trading partner. In this forecast, one early trade succeeds and starts revealing that owl is willing to sell blue for green, hinting that blue may be relatively low value to her compared with red.

After Round 0, some structure emerges. Iguana has successfully acquired extra blue from squid using his cheap red, and owl has traded away some of her blue to squid in exchange for red, revealing that owl is quite red-hungry and views blue as relatively expendable. Squid has become the main counterparty for both, holding a richer mix of colors. Everyone still values green equally, but its role as a neutral currency is now more salient. In Round 1, iguana is expected to push further toward blue, perhaps now willing to part with a bit of green. Owl will continue to chase red and sell blue, and squid might attempt to convert surplus red into higher-value blue, but faces the constraint that iguana and owl may balk at trades that reduce their own emerging strategic positions or look slightly unfair.

By Round 2, patterns are clearer: iguana has heavily concentrated into blue while giving up red and some green, owl has accumulated red by selling blue, and squid has been the main intermediary, often selling blue and buying green or red when it yielded modest gains. Everyone now has some evidence about others’ revealed preferences (especially owl’s strong taste for red and iguana’s drive for blue), but they still lack precise knowledge of each other’s dollar values. Strategic play turns toward fine-tuning: iguana might attempt one more modest blue-acquiring trade, owl might look for a last exchange to pick up red without sacrificing too much, and squid—now with fewer obviously profitable opportunities—may only accept offers that are clearly positive given her valuations. Because it is the final round, there is no need to preserve inventory for future bargaining, so small but strictly positive trades are still attractive if players notice them in time.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip-bargaining game features two high-value color dimensions (RED and BLUE) whose values differ across players and one common-value color (GREEN). Gorilla values BLUE the least and GREEN moderately, making her relatively eager to shed BLUE and moderately willing to trade away RED for more GREEN when it improves her own surplus. Llama and Whale share identical values (RED=$0.60, BLUE=$0.90, GREEN=$0.50), so both strongly prefer keeping or acquiring RED/BLUE over GREEN; they particularly like getting BLUE, which is cheap for Gorilla but very valuable to them. However, no one initially knows others’ private values: they only see their own calculator and must infer others’ motives from observed proposals and acceptances. Gorilla’s high ultimatum rejection rate and medium fairness norms suggest she is selective and wary of exploitative trades, likely requiring clear own-side gains before accepting. Llama and Whale are more tolerant and cooperative, especially Whale who has the lowest minimum acceptable in prior tasks, implying he will accept even modest positive gains. Across three rounds, we expect early, relatively simple exploratory offers (especially Gorilla offering BLUE for RED or GREEN) that reveal relative valuations, followed by more targeted trades where Llama and Whale compete (sometimes both accepting the same attractive offer, leading to random assignment). The tension is between Gorilla’s incentive to exploit her comparative advantage in BLUE versus RED, and Llama/Whale’s desire to convert GREEN into higher-value colored chips without overpaying. Uncertainty remains about how aggressively each party will test the boundaries of acceptability, and some positive-surplus trades may be left on the table if perceived as too one-sided or if players misinfer each other’s values from the small sample of observed moves.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip. The key hidden structure is that Gorilla is the only low-BLUE-value player, while Llama and Whale both prize BLUE and RED highly and value GREEN less. In this opening round, players are still learning, so proposals will likely be simple, single-color-for-single-color trades aimed at testing others’ willingness to pay. Gorilla is likely to try trading away BLUE for RED or GREEN, but without knowing that Llama and Whale both love BLUE, she may choose a conservative, roughly value-neutral offer for herself. Llama and Whale, being more easygoing responders, will tend to accept any trade that is clearly positive for them. Because of symmetric valuations between Llama and Whale, they may sometimes both accept and then rely on the random tie-break, but Gorilla’s first offer will probably be slightly too weak on the recipient side to trigger immediate competition. By the end of the round, at least one clear directional trade that benefits both sides is likely to have occurred, setting up updated beliefs about who values which colors more.

By Round 1, Gorilla has successfully traded away some BLUE for RED to Whale, revealing to Llama and Whale that she is at least somewhat willing to part with BLUE and wants RED. Whale now holds extra RED but fewer BLUE, and both he and Llama know BLUE is very valuable to them. Llama’s first-round attempt to buy BLUE with GREEN was declined, signaling that simply swapping GREEN for BLUE at a mild rate is not appealing—particularly to Gorilla, who values GREEN and dislikes dumping it for BLUE. Players have learned: Gorilla appears BLUE-poor in value terms, Whale is willing to buy BLUE with RED, and GREEN is not a universally cheap commodity. In this round, Gorilla is likely to push harder on her comparative advantage by pitching more attractive BLUE-for-RED offers to entice either Llama or Whale. Llama, having seen Whale already secure a beneficial trade, may become a bit more aggressive in trying to tap Gorilla’s BLUE, while Whale, who is analytically strong and tolerant of mild inequality, will selectively accept trades that are clearly surplus-improving even if slightly skewed in Gorilla’s favor. Competition for Gorilla’s BLUE between Llama and Whale becomes more salient, increasing the chance that multiple acceptances lead to random partner selection.

Entering the final round, Gorilla has twice converted her low-value BLUE into higher-value RED, significantly improving her portfolio. Llama has also gained RED from the Round 1 trade, while Whale gained in Round 0 but did not secure further advantageous trades afterward. All three now have clearer beliefs: everyone sees that Gorilla repeatedly offers BLUE for RED and that Llama and Whale are selective about giving up GREEN and about swapping RED for BLUE when the rate is unfavorable. With only three turns left, players face a classic endgame dynamic: there is no strategic future beyond this game, so each negotiates myopically to maximize immediate surplus. Gorilla, already ahead in surplus terms and risk-averse, is likely to propose one more moderate, safe BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN trade that is clearly beneficial to her without being so extreme that it gets rejected outright. Llama and Whale will only accept if they see a clear gain, but Whale’s lower fairness threshold makes him the most likely final trading partner. Given limited remaining scope for mutually beneficial trades after Gorilla’s earlier gains, some plausible surplus-creating options (particularly GREEN-for-BLUE swaps from Llama/Whale’s perspective) may still not be realized due to lingering asymmetric information and slightly misaligned expectations about what others will accept in a one-shot final move.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately-valued red and blue chips, with tiger placing especially high value on blue and red, lion moderately valuing red and lightly valuing blue, and duck valuing red and green equally while valuing blue relatively low. All start symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color), but not in values. Because players only know their own chip values during play, they must infer others’ valuations from proposal patterns and acceptance behavior over the three rounds.

Key incentives: Tiger has strong motive to acquire blue and red chips and is willing to give up green (which is low relative value for them compared with blue/red). Duck is a cooperative, fairness-oriented player with low rejection thresholds, likely to make generous or at least non-exploitative trades that show visible mutual gain in their own calculator and that they believe might benefit others. Lion has higher minimum acceptable payoffs and is more willing to reject ambiguous or seemingly unfair offers, so lion will usually require clear positive value to accept trades.

Because trades are bilateral but selection is random if multiple recipients accept, proposers must design offers that are good enough for at least one counterpart while respecting their own inventory and ensuring their own net gain. Early in the game, players explore profitable directions: tiger tries to pull blue from others by offering green; lion tries to shift into red from green or blue; duck tries to make small, safe trades that appear win–win and that avoid obvious exploitation. Uncertainty persists because no one directly knows how much others value colors—people must infer, for example, why tiger is so eager to buy blue or why duck is willing to offload blue.

Over the three rounds, the likely pattern is: (1) Round 0 exploratory offers that reveal some structure (e.g., tiger’s strong demand for blue, duck’s willingness to sell blue, lion’s relative caution); (2) Round 1 more targeted trades as players respond to previous acceptances and rejections, with tiger aggressively pursuing blue and red, lion seeking value-improving but not overly risky exchanges, and duck facilitating moderate-scope trades; (3) Round 2 final optimization attempts where players may accept smaller marginal gains (especially duck) or hold out for clearly positive trades (lion and tiger) before the game ends. Random assignment when multiple players accept can subtly skew holdings, but in this forecast, players typically structure offers that are attractive only to one obvious counterpart, reducing this source of risk.

rounds.round_explanation: In Round 0, all players are starting from symmetric inventories and zero history, so their first offers are exploratory and driven mostly by their own valuations and personas rather than strong inferences about others. Duck, being cooperative and fairness-oriented with low rejection thresholds, will likely propose a modest trade that slightly improves their own position without heavily exploiting potential counterpart losses. Lion, more demanding in ultimatum contexts, will seek clearly positive trades and be wary of sacrificing too much value in blue or red. Tiger, with strong private values for blue and red, already has reason to try trading away green (which is relatively less valuable for them) to accumulate more blue and possibly red. Observed acceptances and rejections in this first round start to reveal who is keen to trade which colors.

Entering Round 1, only one trade has succeeded: tiger traded green for blue with duck, revealing that tiger is willing to give up green to gain blue and that duck is willing to accept more green at the expense of some blue. Lion has not traded yet and has seen both lion’s own attempted blue-for-red trade rejected and duck’s blue-for-green attempt rejected, so lion infers that others are cautious about giving up red and perhaps that blue is more valuable to at least one player. Duck now holds extra green and somewhat fewer blue chips, and tiger holds more blue and fewer green. This pushes duck toward offloading some of the surplus green in favor of better-valued colors, and pushes tiger to seek additional blue or opportunistic red while retaining willingness to pay in green. Lion continues to require clear positive value and may try to sell blue (which lion values least) in exchange for green or red but must be careful not to structure a trade that is obviously bad for duck or tiger, who now have more information about how they feel about each color.

By Round 2, successful trades have shifted inventories: duck has swapped some surplus green for red with lion and earlier sold blue for green to tiger; lion has both gained red from duck and sold green to tiger; tiger has gained blue from duck and red from lion in exchange for green. This pattern signals to all players that tiger highly values non-green chips, while duck is comfortable holding green when compensated and lion is actively rebalancing toward red. In the final round, each player faces limited remaining opportunities and may accept smaller marginal gains to improve their final portfolio. Duck, with an empathy and cooperation orientation, is still inclined toward trades that look mutually beneficial and non-exploitative, especially if they fine-tune the green/red/blue mix. Lion remains selective but may accept a clearly beneficial small trade if offered. Tiger, having already made two profitable trades, will still push to convert remaining green into blue or red if the terms are favorable, but with only one proposal left will not risk negative expected value. The resulting pattern is a mix of one last successful trade and some declined attempts that are either too one-sided or misaligned with the remaining chip needs of potential counterparties.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-player bargaining game revolves around converting low-value chips into high-value chips given each player’s private values. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it acts as a safe, mutually transparent benchmark. Red is worth $1.00 to bear and goose but only $0.80 to ram; blue is worth $1.00 to ram, $0.30 to bear, and $0.20 to goose. Thus, ram is the blue specialist, and bear/goose are red specialists, with everyone valuing green the same. High-gain trades move blue from bear/goose to ram and red from ram to bear/goose, often using green as a buffer. However, players do not know others’ exact values; they only see their own calculator and aggregate behavior over time. Bear, who is older and somewhat generous in ultimatum tasks, is likely to suggest moderately fair trades that definitely increase his own surplus but leave some slack for partners. Goose and ram both showed very low rejection thresholds in ultimatum tasks, so they are inclined to accept almost any strictly beneficial deal, even small gains. The main uncertainties are about how valuable blue and red are to others and whether two responders may simultaneously accept, triggering random partner selection. This risk discourages proposers from offering extremely generous “obvious bargains” that both other players would eagerly snap up. Over three short rounds, players have limited time to learn valuations, so they rely on simple, clearly profitable exchanges and avoid complicated or marginal proposals. The likely pattern is that early trades are conservative but positive-sum, with later trades becoming slightly more targeted once players infer who seems eager to trade specific colors.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each color and only know their own values. No one has seen any behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ values are diffuse. The obvious structural clue is that blue and red ranges are wide, while green is fixed at $0.50. Bear knows red is very valuable to him and blue is weak; goose similarly knows red dominates blue; ram knows blue dominates red. But none know that others share or invert these rankings. Given bear’s relatively generous but self-protective profile, his first move is likely a simple one-hop improvement for himself that is not so extreme that two responders obviously gain a lot, to avoid randomization. Goose, with extremely low rejection thresholds, will tend to accept anything that clearly helps her and costs little. Ram is highly numerate and sees big upside in acquiring blue; he will eagerly accept trades that give him blue at a favorable effective price, even if small. Round 0 therefore likely features at least one successful red–blue trade involving ram as a recipient, establishing a first hint that he values blue strongly.

After round 0, two trades have executed. First, bear traded 1 green for 2 blue with ram, improving both: bear cheaply tested demand for blue and ram gained surplus by giving up a common-value chip for strong blue. This signals to both goose and bear that ram willingly trades away green and must value blue quite a bit. Second, ram traded 2 blue for 3 red with bear, a strongly positive swap for both given their valuations. Now bear holds more high-value red and reasonable blue; ram has even more blue and less red; goose has not traded yet and still sits on a symmetric 10–10–10 endowment. Strategically, bear now knows at least one partner is very eager for blue relative to green and red, and ram infers bear values red highly. Goose sees she might be behind but appears comfortable accepting small gains and may try to catch up by proposing something modestly favorable to herself that someone will take. In this middle round, players are more willing to make slightly larger, but still obviously profitable, color swaps, especially involving red-for-blue or red-for-green bundles that leverage the now-revealed complementarity between bear and ram.

Going into the final round, trade history suggests: ram pays willingly in green for blue, and also in blue for red; bear readily accepts red for green when the rate is favorable; goose finally succeeded in a modest red-for-green trade and appears very tolerant of low gains. Holdings are no longer symmetric, and each player now has a rough map: bear is red-loving, ram is blue-loving, and goose seems somewhat flexible but has benefitted mostly by acquiring red via bear. With only three turns left, incentives shift toward locking in any last positive-sum swaps without worrying much about future signaling. Players avoid trades that risk big losses or that give too much surplus away, but they are willing to take small, safe gains even if lopsided, especially goose and ram who have very low rejection thresholds. Final trades are likely simple and bilateral: more red–blue or red–green exchanges that fine-tune each player’s portfolio toward their high-value colors, with at least one small deal failing because it is too tilted or because the target partner is now chip-constrained on the color being sold.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values on RED and BLUE chips and a common value for GREEN. GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone, so trading GREEN only helps if it reliably pulls in higher-value RED/BLUE from the other side. OCTOPUS has the richest pattern: both RED and BLUE are worth $1.00, so he is strongly motivated to acquire either color and reluctant to give them up unless well compensated. LION values BLUE at $0.70 and RED/GREEN at $0.50, so she has mild motive to convert some RED into BLUE, but not at big discounts. RAM values BLUE like LION ($0.70), GREEN at $0.50, and RED at just $0.20, and thus is the most eager seller of RED and relatively keen on acquiring BLUE/ GREEN. However, players only see their own chip values; they must infer others’ values from observed trades. The main efficient pattern, if discovered, is RED flowing from RAM to OCTOPUS (and possibly to LION) and BLUE flowing out of OCTOPUS (to LION and RAM), often using GREEN or low-valued RED as sweeteners. With three rounds and one proposal per player per round, there is enough time for some learning, but risk of coordination failure remains. Personas suggest all three have low rejection thresholds in ultimatum tasks, so they likely accept any trade with a clear positive private surplus, even if it seems uneven. OCTOPUS and LION are moderately cooperative and numerate; RAM is more self-focused but still responsive to clear gains. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others’ valuations or fairness norms and from the random partner selection if multiple recipients accept, which discourages highly targeted proposals and pushes senders toward deals that are attractive to at least one likely counterparty without being so generous that everyone always jumps in.

rounds.round_explanation: Everyone starts at symmetric holdings (10 of each color) but with different private values. No one knows others’ valuations yet, so the first round is largely exploratory. Rational proposers will draft trades that guarantee themselves a positive surplus given their own values and test whether someone bites. Given low rejection thresholds, responders will accept almost any strictly beneficial deal. The likely first patterns: RAM trying to unload RED for BLUE or GREEN, OCTOPUS seeking to buy RED or BLUE while paying in GREEN, and LION cautiously probing small BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN swaps. Accepted early deals will give clues about who values which colors, shaping later proposals.

After round 0, RAM has parted with several RED to OCTOPUS and gained GREEN, and also traded some RED for BLUE with LION. Publicly, everyone can see that RAM is repeatedly willing to give up RED, hinting that RED might be low-value for him. OCTOPUS acquiring multiple RED for GREEN signals he likely values RED at least as much as GREEN. LION’s willingness to give up RED for BLUE suggests BLUE is at least moderately more attractive to her. In round 1, proposers can refine: OCTOPUS should try again to buy RED from RAM, possibly at slightly worse terms for RAM; LION might again offer RED for BLUE but will be cautious not to overpay; RAM, seeing low uptake in his own proposal, may adjust or temporarily hold off. Some mutually beneficial trades remain, especially RED-to-OCTOPUS and BLUE-to-LION, but thin margins and uncertainty about how aggressive others will be can cause stalled or conservative offers.

By the start of round 2, patterns are clearer: OCTOPUS has consistently bought RED from RAM using GREEN, indicating strong valuation of RED; RAM has repeatedly sold RED and occasionally traded GREEN for BLUE, revealing his preference ordering BLUE ≥ GREEN ≫ RED; LION has pursued BLUE, swapping out both RED and GREEN at times. Remaining gains from trade are mainly incremental: RAM can still profitably unload any remaining RED to OCTOPUS if OCTOPUS is willing, and minor BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN trades with LION could still help both sides. However, inventories are thinner and all players are more aware of each other’s strategies, which can harden bargaining stances. Final-round dynamics often feature either one more clear win–win trade, typically again RED from RAM to OCTOPUS, or a sequence of rejected offers if proposers push too close to the margin where counterparts are indifferent or wary of being exploited.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric 10/10/10 chip inventories but markedly different private values for red and blue chips. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 each for everyone.

Private values:
- buffalo-purple-5232 (buffalo): RED $0.60, BLUE $0.10, GREEN $0.50
- cat-yellow-2069 (cat): RED $1.00, BLUE $1.00, GREEN $0.50
- duck-green-2458 (duck): RED $1.00, BLUE $0.70, GREEN $0.50

From an analyst perspective, buffalo has a strong incentive to convert low-value BLUE into higher-value RED or GREEN, but they do not know that others especially value RED and BLUE. Cat values RED and BLUE equally and highly; they are happy to accumulate either from others at modest terms. Duck is numerate and low in rejection rate; he tends to accept any trade with even a small positive gain in his own calculator. Cat’s ultimatum profile suggests medium thresholds for fairness, so she is less likely than duck to accept trades with very small gains or visible losses, but will still respond to clear positive surplus. Buffalo is cooperative and low on rejection thresholds, so as a responder she will often accept modestly profitable trades.

Key strategic features:
- Proposers see only their own valuations, so every offer will be personally profitable ex ante. They may unintentionally leave large surplus on the table because they cannot tailor offers to others’ private values.
- Responders see their own net change on-screen for each posted proposal, so acceptance decisions are largely myopic and value-based, with some fairness sensitivity for cat.
- Because multiple acceptances trigger a random recipient draw, proposers must worry that too-generous offers might be snapped up by whichever responder happens to benefit the most, without the proposer being able to choose who they trade with.
- Over three short rounds with fixed proposer order, players have little time to learn others’ values, so most behavior remains myopic-profit oriented rather than fully strategic.

Given these incentives and personalities, we expect:
- buffalo to probe with conservative, personally profitable trades that try to move BLUE out and bring in RED or GREEN, sometimes slightly underestimating how good the deal looks to others.
- cat to be an active proposer trying to upgrade low-value GREEN into high-value RED or BLUE, and as a responder to accept clearly positive changes but occasionally reject borderline deals.
- duck to calculate carefully and accept almost any proposal with a non-trivial positive gain, especially ones that let him pick up RED cheaply.
Overall, the game should see a few successful trades, mostly involving players with low fairness thresholds (buffalo and duck) while cat acts as a somewhat choosier bargainer.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still hold 10 of each chip and have no direct information about others’ private values. Proposals are primarily guided by each proposer’s own valuations and generic fairness intuitions. buffalo will likely start cautiously, aiming for a mild gain without appearing too exploitative. cat will then try to convert common-value GREEN into higher-value chips (RED or BLUE) on terms that look clearly beneficial. duck, being very acceptance-prone and analytically strong, will be ready to accept anything that delivers clear surplus, especially more RED at low cost.

After round 0, buffalo has shifted some BLUE into GREEN, cat has traded some GREEN for RED with duck, and duck has both gained BLUE from buffalo and lost some GREEN and RED to cat. Everyone now has slightly skewed inventories but still no direct knowledge of each other’s valuations—only weak hints that duck is relatively willing to accept and that cat is selective. Proposers in round 1 adjust mildly: buffalo continues trying to offload BLUE for more valuable chips, cat experiments with a somewhat more generous offer to entice acceptance, and duck, having seen cat accept a profitable trade, crafts a deal aimed at buffalo, whom he suspects may undervalue BLUE.

Entering the final round, players have a clearer sense of who tends to accept which types of trades. buffalo has repeatedly managed to turn low-value BLUE into GREEN, mostly via duck. cat has gained both RED and BLUE via favorable trades with duck and is now strongly ahead in total value. duck, while numerically savvy, has been the net donor of surplus so far; he is now more cautious, especially about giving up RED. With only three turns left, buffalo tries once more to monetize remaining BLUE, cat aims for a last small upgrade that someone might still accept, and duck proposes a conservative trade seeking incremental value without creating obvious losses for others—though by now both potential partners are wary of sacrificing value in the final moves.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips, with common-value green chips. Butterfly and Squid both value red and blue chips highly and fairly similarly (Butterfly: RED 1.00, BLUE 0.90; Squid: RED 0.90, BLUE 0.80), while Kangaroo has much lower values, especially for blue (RED 0.40, BLUE 0.10). Green is worth 0.50 to everyone. This makes Kangaroo a natural low-value "broker" whose chips can be profitably bought by either Butterfly or Squid. However, no one knows others' exact values; each only knows their own, plus the public ranges, so they must infer who is high- vs low-value from patterns of offers and acceptances.

Incentives: Butterfly and Squid want to convert low-value holdings into higher-value ones at favorable rates. Given their high valuations, they are often willing to pay Kangaroo more green or some red/blue to acquire Kangaroo's under-valued reds and especially blues, as long as their own surplus increase is positive. Kangaroo, by contrast, benefits by trading away red and blue when offered enough green or higher-valued color from their own perspective. Given Kangaroo's very low blue value, they are especially happy to sell blue for almost anything that yields a positive gain. Strategic tension arises because each proposer must choose a partner and a rate without knowing others' exact valuations or how choosy they are.

Game structure and uncertainty: There are three rounds with three proposal turns each (nine proposals total). Orders of moves are fixed (Butterfly, then Kangaroo, then Squid each round). Offers must involve giving some of one color in exchange for some of another color. Responders see exactly how a trade affects their own payoff via the surplus calculator. Thus, conditional on an offer, responders act almost purely to maximize own value: they accept whenever the proposal yields a non-negative (most likely positive) net gain and reject otherwise. The main uncertainty is about others' values and behavioral tendencies, not about whether they correctly understand a specific trade's consequence.

Persona influences: Butterfly is cooperative and tolerant, with a history of almost never rejecting ultimatum offers and being willing to accept very low payoffs; she is likely to avoid extremely exploitative proposals and to accept even modestly beneficial ones. Kangaroo is more self-focused and previously offered very little in ultimatum, though still with a relatively low rejection threshold; she may craft more self-favoring offers and be selective but not hyper-punitive. Squid is both prosocial and somewhat demanding, with a history of offering and expecting medium fairness and rejecting unfair splits relatively often; she is likely to pursue surplus-increasing trades but may decline deals that feel too one-sided, even if slightly profitable.

Over the three rounds, we should expect (1) early, somewhat exploratory offers that test others' willingness to trade, (2) emerging recognition that Kangaroo is often the most profitable counterparty for Butterfly and Squid, and (3) some bilateral trade patterns where high-value players buy from Kangaroo. Because trades execute as soon as one responder accepts and the partner is not chosen by the proposer when multiple accept, offers that appeal to both non-senders may create random-matching risk: a proposer might end up trading with the less preferred partner. This encourages targeted offers where the terms are especially attractive to a single intended recipient. Given the value structure, many surplus-improving trades revolve around higher-value players buying Kangaroo's blue and red, paid with green or with colors Kangaroo values more than blue.

rounds.round_explanation: In Round 0, all players still have symmetric 10-10-10 endowments. No one has observed any behavior, so beliefs about others' values are largely prior-based. Butterfly, moving first, is likely to make a cautious, modestly surplus-creating offer that seems fair and tests market interest, especially toward the apparently low-value counterpart (suspected to be Kangaroo) without overcommitting chips. Kangaroo, moving second, has already seen Butterfly's first proposal and any acceptances, giving some evidence about others' demand for red/blue vs green; she is likely to test selling her low-valued blue or moderate-valued red in exchange for green. Squid, moving last, responds after observing both prior turns and will typically look for a clearly profitable, possibly more balanced trade, especially with Kangaroo, to exploit value differences while maintaining a sense of fairness.

By Round 1, players have observed that Kangaroo was willing to sell blue for green and then sell red for green, and that both Butterfly and Squid accepted moderately favorable but not extreme offers. This reveals: (a) Kangaroo likely values blue and red relatively low versus green; (b) Butterfly will accept trades with modest gains; and (c) Squid is willing to use green to buy red when the rate is slightly favorable. Holdings are now asymmetric, so strategic focus shifts toward consolidating high-value colors for each player. Butterfly, moving first again, can exploit her strong value for red and blue by selling some green to either partner in exchange for high-value red or blue. Kangaroo will likely continue offloading low-valued blue or red for green or slightly better-valued colors, perhaps adjusting rates upward after seeing initial acceptances. Squid, seeing two rounds of behavior, may make more targeted proposals aimed at Kangaroo, balancing fairness with clear surplus gains.

In Round 2, players recognize it is the final set of proposals, so there is no reason to preserve chips for future bargaining. Each proposer focuses purely on end-game portfolio optimization: convert any relatively low-valued colors into higher-valued ones given what counterparties seem willing to trade. Butterfly has shown a pattern of buying colored chips from Kangaroo using green; Kangaroo has signaled a willingness to sell both red and blue for green and possibly accept slightly worse terms if still profitable; Squid has positioned herself as a relatively demanding but active buyer of red and blue. This round will likely feature slightly more aggressive proposals that push exchange rates further in favor of the proposer, while still staying inside respondents' acceptance thresholds as revealed by the surplus calculator and prior behavior.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players begin with symmetric endowments (10 of each color), but their marginal valuations differ: frog-blue-6296 values blue highest ($1.00) and red moderately ($0.50); fox-green-2854 values blue at $0.90 and red at $0.40; bat-pink-1751 values red and blue equally at $0.80. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone and are therefore natural "cash-like" mediators. Each player can only see their own valuations, so they know others' preferences only through rational inference and observed behavior over the three rounds. Incentives center on exploiting value differences: high-blue players want to acquire more blue even at cost of low-value colors, and low-red players may profit by selling red to high-red or equal-red players.

Because acceptance is simultaneous and multiple acceptances are randomly resolved, proposers face uncertainty about which counterparty they will trade with. This discourages highly targeted deals and pushes toward proposals that are at least weakly beneficial to both potential recipients. The surplus calculator makes within-player optimization straightforward: nobody will knowingly accept a net-loss trade, and proposers will tend to pick offers that yield a clear positive surplus for themselves while (they hope) being good enough for at least one partner to accept.

The personas suggest all three have very low rejection thresholds in simple ultimatum games and are not inclined to punish unfairness. Combined with reasonable numeracy and the calculator, this makes it likely they will accept any strictly profitable trade, even if the gain is small or the split of surplus is unequal. Over only three rounds (nine total proposal turns), dynamics will be myopic rather than deeply strategic: each player tries to grab locally positive expected value whenever available, with limited concern for future retaliation or reputation. Early accepted trades will reveal some coarse information about relative valuations (for example, someone eagerly trading away red may hint that red is relatively low value to them), which can shape slightly more targeted proposals in later rounds. Still, short horizon and random recipient resolution keep the environment noisy, so most proposals remain simple, symmetric-looking exchanges that plausibly benefit either non-sender.

Given the specific valuations, frog-blue-6296 has the strongest motive to acquire blue and is willing to pay with green or red; fox-green-2854 also favors blue over red but has less spread between them; bat-pink-1751 values red and blue equally and green slightly less, making her roughly indifferent between red and blue and willing to trade green away for either. Efficient trades generally move blue chips toward frog and fox, and red chips away from fox. However, because nobody directly observes others' values, we forecast modest, roughly balanced trades: 1–3 chips per deal, often involving green as the selling color and blue or red as the purchased color. Across the three rounds, we expect a small number of accepted trades that gradually shift blue chips toward frog and fox and red chips slightly toward bat, with occasional declined offers when the proposer misjudges what will be attractive to at least one counterpart.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players start with symmetric inventories (10 of each color) and no information about others' valuations. Proposals are exploratory, guided by each proposer's own private values and a desire to create deals that look modest and fair to either potential recipient. Because all three have low rejection tendencies and use the calculator, any proposal that offers receivers clear positive value should be attractive. However, proposers still worry about asking for too many high-value chips and getting no acceptance. Round 0 is therefore characterized by small, conservative trades designed to test the waters and reveal a bit about who is willing to shed which colors.

After round 0, frog-blue-6296 has revealed a clear willingness to trade away blue for green and red, signaling that blue might be especially valuable to him. Bat and fox have both seen that modest, roughly equal-looking exchanges can succeed. Inventories are now slightly asymmetric, so round 1 proposals account for updated holdings: frog has fewer blue and more red/green; bat has more blue and less green; fox has fewer red and slightly more blue. Players still lack precise knowledge of others' valuations, but they can infer that blue is actively demanded and that red may be relatively cheap for fox. Round 1 is dominated by attempts to exploit these emerging patterns—especially moves that trade away colors each sender likely values less in exchange for colors they personally favor—while staying within a small scale to maintain high acceptance probabilities.

By round 2, only a few proposal opportunities remain, so players focus on final adjustments to their portfolios. Frog has consistently acquired blue and disposed of some red and green; fox has shifted some red away and picked up blue and green; bat has converted green into red and blue. The observed pattern suggests to everyone that blue is widely valued, red is somewhat weaker for fox, and green is safe trade currency. Given the short horizon, there is little incentive to hold out for better future terms; each player now looks for any remaining trades that give them a clear positive surplus and that are simple enough to be accepted by at least one partner. Round 2 thus features relatively small, tidy exchanges that fine-tune holdings: frog still chases blue when possible, fox is willing to give up red for either green or blue, and bat continues to spend green to balance red and blue holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN, with GREEN having a common known value of $0.50 to everyone. The key private uncertainty is about how much each other player values RED and BLUE. In reality, parrot and turtle both value BLUE at $1.00, while dog values BLUE only at $0.30. For RED, parrot values it at $0.80, turtle at $0.60, and dog at $0.50. This means dog is the low-BLUE, medium-RED player; parrot is high on both RED and BLUE; turtle is high-BLUE, medium-RED. From an efficiency perspective, BLUE should flow away from dog toward parrot and turtle, and some RED could flow away from dog toward parrot. Because GREEN is valued the same by everyone, it serves as the natural "currency" for trading, and fair-looking offers will often be framed as exchanging GREEN for more valuable colored chips from the responder's perspective. However, players only see their own valuations, so they must infer others’ preferences from the pattern of offers and acceptances over turns. Dog’s ultimatum profile (very low offer and low minimum acceptable) suggests high flexibility and low concern about fairness norms; dog is likely to focus on self-gain and to accept many small positive trades. Parrot appears generous and tolerant in ultimatum but with high uncertainty aversion, so they are likely to avoid trades that show an immediate loss on their calculator, but are willing to share surplus when proposing. Turtle’s medium minimum acceptable and higher rejection rate suggest they are more selective and more willing to reject low-gain or ambiguous trades. Across three rounds, each player proposes three times. Early offers are likely small and exploratory, using GREEN as payment to probe whether others value RED or BLUE more. As they see which deals are accepted—especially offers where responders give up particular colors for GREEN—they update beliefs about others’ values and adjust terms. Random selection when multiple players accept creates an additional incentive for proposers to structure offers that are attractive mainly to the counterpart they most want to trade with, but this is hard because they cannot see others’ calculators. Given the short horizon (only three rounds), players are biased toward immediately profitable trades rather than long sequences of position-building. The likely pattern is: dog tries to cheaply acquire RED using BLUE or GREEN, underestimating others’ taste for BLUE; parrot aggressively accumulates BLUE and some RED using GREEN; turtle selectively sells RED for GREEN when the rate is clearly favorable but resists giving up too much BLUE. Many proposals that look good to the proposer will be declined because responders see a loss or trivial gain; accepted trades will typically show at least a modest positive dollar gain for both sides once each applies their own private valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, everyone still has symmetric inventories and no information about others’ private values beyond priors. Proposals are likely to be small and exploratory, testing whether others will sell specific colors for GREEN. Dog, who is flexible and not fairness-focused, may start with a self-favoring but not outrageous rate using GREEN as payment. Parrot will likely propose trades that slightly favor themselves but still give responders a clear calculator gain, aiming to discover who is willing to sell RED or BLUE. Turtle, more selective, will probably make offers that guarantee a solid personal gain and may prefer acquiring BLUE with GREEN, expecting that others undervalue BLUE. Given no prior trade history, some offers will be declined as responders protect themselves from even small apparent losses.

By the start of round 1, each player has seen which early probes were accepted, giving them some noisy signals about others’ valuations. Dog has learned that parrot is willing to part with RED at a 1:1 rate for GREEN, and that turtle was not; parrot has seen that turtle sells BLUE for GREEN at parity but dog does not; turtle has observed that dog buys RED for GREEN. These patterns suggest to parrot that both others may value GREEN at least as much as their traded colors, reinforcing GREEN as a medium. Dog is now slightly heavier in RED and GREEN and lighter in BLUE, and may seek additional RED from turtle, expecting similar willingness to trade for GREEN, though turtle’s earlier decline signals caution. Parrot, who now holds more BLUE but fewer GREEN, may offer RED or BLUE to rebuild GREEN while still maintaining surplus. Turtle, enriched in BLUE but slightly down in RED and GREEN, is likely to capitalize on BLUE’s apparent attractiveness by asking more GREEN per BLUE or to continue selling RED only at clearly favorable rates. Players will start to shade terms more aggressively in their favor now that they have some idea of who trades what.

Entering the final round, all players recognize that this is the last chance to improve their chip portfolios. Dog has repeatedly accepted trades that yield clear calculator gains, even if small, and is now relatively rich in GREEN and somewhat balanced in RED and BLUE. Parrot has concentrated in high-value BLUE and RED but is low on GREEN, making them willing to sell some BLUE or RED for GREEN at favorable rates. Given the end-game nature, there is no incentive to hold back for future rounds; players will propose bolder, more value-extractive trades that still leave responders at least marginally better off on their calculators. Dog is likely to propose acquiring more RED from parrot using GREEN; parrot may attempt a final BLUE-for-GREEN trade toward whoever still accepts such deals; turtle may attempt to offload a little more RED for GREEN or, if sensing strong demand for BLUE, to sell a single BLUE at a high GREEN multiple, trusting their own higher valuation but exploiting dog’s and parrot’s eagerness.
