parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game features strong but asymmetric private values on red and blue chips, with green as a common-value medium chip. All start with 10 of each color (R,B,G). Valuations:
- Badger: R=$0.60, B=$0.90, G=$0.50 (strong blue buyer vs both others; red moderate; green baseline)
- Peacock: R=$1.00, B=$0.10, G=$0.50 (strong red buyer, blue almost worthless, green baseline)
- Unicorn: R=$0.40, B=$1.00, G=$0.50 (strong blue buyer; red relatively weak; green baseline)

Key cross-player value differences (per chip):
- Badger vs Peacock: B is +$0.80 more valuable to Badger; R is +$0.40 more valuable to Peacock.
- Badger vs Unicorn: B is +$0.10 more valuable to Unicorn; R is +$0.20 more valuable to Badger.
- Peacock vs Unicorn: R is +$0.60 more valuable to Peacock; B is +$0.90 more valuable to Unicorn.

This makes two particularly profitable trade directions if discovered:
1) Peacock ↔ Unicorn: Peacock sells blue (cheap to him) for red (valuable to him); Unicorn sells red (moderately valued) for blue (very valuable). Surplus per chip can easily exceed $0.75.
2) Badger ↔ Peacock: Badger sells red (moderate) for blue (very valuable) against a counterparty who sees the opposite. Or Badger ↔ Peacock via green as a neutral medium.

However, each player initially sees only their own valuations and must infer others' preferences from revealed behavior within just 3 rounds (9 total turns). With simultaneous responses and random recipient selection when multiple players accept, proposers cannot target a specific partner, which creates strategic uncertainty: efficient deals that benefit both Badger and Peacock might accidentally go to Unicorn, who may value the chips differently. Because all have surplus calculators, responders will usually accept any proposal that gives them non-negative expected value in their own units, but may occasionally accept very small positive gains due to fairness or cooperation norms.

Personas imply:
- Badger: relatively cooperative, low rejection thresholds; willing to accept modest gains and propose somewhat fair, not ultra-aggressive, trades. High uncertainty aversion suggests avoiding complicated or borderline proposals.
- Peacock: highly strategic and numerate, with medium fairness thresholds and moderate rejection propensity. Likely to search for clearly positive-sum trades and avoid giving others too much surplus.
- Unicorn: has a history of accepting even very low shares and rarely rejecting in ultimatum games; likely to accept almost any positive-value trade and may propose quite self-serving deals but is also highly cooperative, potentially tolerating small personal gains if beneficial to others.

Given only 3 rounds and no enforceable long-term relationship, players will likely prioritize clear private gains over exploring others' values. Expect the most transparent mispricing—Peacock’s cheap blue and Unicorn’s cheap red—to be exploited if noticed, generating blue-for-red swaps. Badger will probably focus on acquiring blue and unloading some red, but may get fewer trades if Peacock and Unicorn quickly discover their very strong red/blue complementarity and primarily trade with each other. Some profitable green-for-red or green-for-blue trades may appear as simple, low-risk experiments, especially early on when uncertainty about others’ values is highest.

Overall forecast: at least a couple of accepted trades, concentrated on red/blue swaps involving Peacock and Unicorn; Badger secures at least one modestly profitable trade but may miss the highest-surplus opportunities among the other two. Strategic uncertainty about who will accept and random recipient selection will occasionally cause a proposer’s intended partner to miss out, but responders’ low rejection thresholds mean most clearly positive proposals to at least one non-sender will tend to be accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: At the beginning of round 0, all players hold 10R/10B/10G. No one has yet seen others’ behavior, so each proposer is essentially probing the market with simple, easy-to-evaluate trades. They know their own relative values but not others’. Likely early moves: Badger experiments with trading away red (medium value) for blue (high value) or green (neutral), offering responders strictly positive or close-to-neutral gains. Peacock, noticing his own near-zero value for blue and high value for red, likely tests a blue-for-red or blue-for-green proposal that is mildly favorable to any counterparty. Unicorn, whose blue is very valuable and red mediocre, may either try speculative small trades or accept many of others’ offers to gather surplus with low risk. Because everyone can see net gains on-screen, clearly loss-making trades will be rejected; however, low but positive gains may still be accepted, especially by Unicorn and Badger.

By the start of round 1, the history reveals: (1) Badger traded 2R for 2B with Peacock, signaling Badger values blue more than red and that Peacock is at least mildly willing to give up blue for red; and (2) Peacock traded 1R for 3B with Unicorn, signaling Peacock’s blue is very cheap to him and that Unicorn is happy to acquire blue even when giving up some red. Unicorn’s declined proposal to swap 1B for 2R shows Badger and Peacock both saw that as unprofitable given their own values. From this, players can infer a rough pattern: Peacock strongly prefers red to blue, Unicorn strongly prefers blue to red, and Badger also prefers blue to red but not as extremely as Unicorn. Strategically, Badger should look for further blue acquisitions, particularly from Peacock, while Peacock and Unicorn should try to deepen their high-surplus blue-for-red exchanges. However, with limited time remaining, proposers may focus on straightforward, one-step improvements rather than riskier or more complex offers.

Going into the final round, substantial red–blue reallocation has already occurred. The pattern is clear to all: Peacock consistently sells blue to get red, Unicorn consistently sells red to get blue, and Badger has repeatedly sold red to acquire blue from Peacock. Each player now has stronger beliefs about others’ relative values, so remaining proposals are likely to be more aggressive: low-risk, high-surplus swaps that lock in final incremental gains. With only three turns left, there is no future-round reputation concern; proposers will push for deals that yield strictly positive value for at least one responder, while responders will reject any clear losses but accept even modest positive gains. This leads to one more profitable Badger–Peacock exchange, a large final blue-for-red consolidation between Peacock and Unicorn, and a cautious but ultimately unappealing green-mediated attempt by Unicorn that is rejected by both counterparts.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately-valued red and blue chips. All start with 10 of each color. The privately known values are: dog: RED=$0.50, BLUE=$0.30; mouse: RED=$0.40, BLUE=$0.40; owl: RED=$0.50, BLUE=$0.70. Thus, owl has by far the highest value for blue, dog and owl tie for red, and mouse is weakest on both red and blue but symmetric between them. From a social-surplus standpoint, efficient reallocations would move blue chips toward owl and red chips toward dog or owl, and potentially move some red/blue away from mouse.

However, each player only knows their own payoffs. They see that all chips have some positive value and that green has a narrow public range (exactly $0.50), whereas red/blue ranges are wider and less certain. This creates uncertainty about others’ valuations: owl likely suspects they like blue a lot but does not know that others like blue much less; dog knows blue is their weakest chip and will infer that others may value it more; mouse may view red and blue as roughly interchangeable for themselves but cannot see that owl values blue far more.

The key strategic forces:
- Dog is highly cooperative and tolerant of low personal gains (based on ultimatum behavior). Dog is likely to make fair or even generous offers, avoid visibly exploitative deals, and accept many trades that give them even small gains. Dog’s main economic incentive, given their valuations, is to shed blue (low $0.30) for red or green ($0.50), but their fairness orientation might lead to near-even value splits.
- Mouse is cooperative but somewhat more self-protective and numerate. Their economically rational move is to acquire more of whichever color is especially valuable to others (once they infer this) and trade away the color that is less in demand. Early, they may be more exploratory, using green as a neutral medium because its value is common knowledge.
- Owl is the most analytically strategic and has a very strong incentive to accumulate blue, which is worth $0.70 to them. They will actively look for opportunities to buy blue cheaply in terms of value, especially from dog, for whom blue is relatively weak. Because they are fair-minded but less generous than dog, they may attempt slightly asymmetric but not blatantly unfair deals that others will still accept.

Bargaining dynamics over the three rounds likely follow this path: Round 0 is exploratory and fairness-anchored; players test simple small-size trades, especially involving green because its value is known by all. Dog may reveal a willingness to engage in low-margin but kind trades. Mouse and owl watch each other’s and dog’s revealed preferences—who eagerly buys or sells particular colors—to infer valuations. Round 1 sees more targeted trades: once owl or mouse suspect that dog undervalues blue relative to them, they may offer dog red or green for blue. Players also face the constraint of one proposal per turn and a total of only three rounds; because the horizon is short, they will accept any clearly positive-value deal that doesn’t seem exploitative. Round 2 is effectively a closing phase: each player, especially owl, tries to make a final consolidation move toward their best-valued chips, while dog continues to accept modestly beneficial offers.

Given simultaneous private responses and random selection among multiple acceptors, proposers cannot choose partners, which slightly discourages making highly skewed deals that might attract multiple acceptances (creating risk of trading with the wrong partner). Because everyone sees their own net gain or loss before deciding, accepted trades are assumed to be weakly value-improving for both parties. Across the full game, we therefore expect a moderate but not fully efficient concentration of blue in owl’s hands and some movement of blue out of dog’s inventory, with green and red sometimes serving as fair-looking payment media. Mouse ends with modestly improved holdings but is less central to the most surplus-generating blue-for-nonblue trades.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is exploratory. No one yet knows others’ relative valuations, and all have symmetric endowments. Because green’s value is common knowledge ($0.50), it is a natural baseline for early trades. Dog’s fairness and cooperativeness incline them to propose gentle, near-fair trades that might reveal willingness to swap low-value chips for higher-value ones from their own perspective (especially turning blue into red/green). Mouse and owl are attentive: owl is looking for signs that others do not value blue highly, while mouse is assessing which colors draw more interest. Early trades are small in size to limit risk and to probe acceptance thresholds.

By Round 1, some information has been revealed through accepted and declined offers. Dog’s initial willingness to trade blue for green suggests to others that blue may not be especially valued by dog, while mouse’s acceptance of owl’s blue-for-green trade signals that mouse is at least neutral about swapping green for blue. Owl starts to form a hypothesis that they may like blue more than others and can profitably accumulate it. Players now begin to make more strategically targeted proposals: dog continues to trade away blue for higher-valued chips, owl explicitly tries to buy blue using green or red, and mouse experiments with leveraging their neutral stance on red vs. blue to secure small gains. Fairness concerns remain important, especially for dog, but with only two rounds left after this one, everyone is more willing to accept modest positive-value deals.

Round 2 is the final opportunity to reallocate chips. At this point, owl has successfully added blue and shown repeated willingness to trade away red or green for blue, which dog has repeatedly accepted when it yields them more valuable chips. Mouse has engaged in modest value-improving trades but has not specialized aggressively toward any color. Everyone now knows that others are broadly willing to accept mutual-gain trades, and very unfair or loss-making offers are unlikely to be proposed at all because the surplus calculator would flag them as losses to the responder.

Strategically, dog aims for a last chance to convert any remaining low-value blue into red or green, even with only small incremental gains, consistent with their cooperative profile. Mouse tries for a final, small surplus gain by swapping into whichever color they have slightly less of and expects to be relatively valued by at least one counterparty. Owl makes one more attempt to buy blue cheaply from dog, potentially in slightly larger quantity, balancing fairness against their strong value advantage on blue. Since it is the final round, all players are more willing to accept any clear positive-value trade; there is no need to preserve flexibility for future rounds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with identical chip holdings (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but slightly different private dollar values. Alligator and Buffalo value RED at $1.00 and BLUE at $0.70; Rabbit values RED at $0.90 and BLUE at $0.60. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Thus, RED dominates BLUE and GREEN for Alligator and Buffalo, and RED also dominates for Rabbit, but by a slightly smaller margin. BLUE is always strictly worse than RED for everyone and weakly better than GREEN for Alligator and Buffalo, while BLUE equals GREEN’s value for Rabbit. Efficient trades must move chips from lower-value uses to higher-value uses, while still giving each side a private surplus gain. However, because valuations are relatively close and symmetric across players, there are few large-gain opportunities. The main asymmetry is that Alligator and Buffalo see BLUE strictly above GREEN, while Rabbit is indifferent between BLUE and GREEN but values RED slightly less. This creates small gains from trade, for example, Rabbit can cheaply give up BLUE or GREEN in exchange for RED from Alligator or Buffalo, as long as the RED/BLUE or RED/GREEN rate is favorable to both sides. Strategic uncertainty arises from hidden valuations and three-player competition: a proposer does not know others’ exact dollar values, only public ranges, so they must guess what rates others find acceptable while also recognizing that any attractive deal may be accepted by multiple responders, with the partner randomly chosen. Personas matter: Alligator is tolerant of very asymmetric deals and unlikely to reject small gains; Buffalo is more fairness-sensitive and more willing to reject low-perceived-value proposals; Rabbit is analytically strong and easy-going on minimum acceptable payoffs. Over three short rounds, proposers are incentivized to make simple, obviously beneficial trades that at least one responder will accept, while responders focus on their personal surplus and may compete to grab favorable offers. The likely pattern is: early probing offers to test what looks acceptable, followed by slightly more targeted, surplus-sharing trades that tend to move RED toward Rabbit (who can acquire it slightly cheaper in terms of BLUE/GREEN) and possibly GREEN or BLUE back toward Alligator and Buffalo, but without dramatic specialization because time is short and surplus margins are thin.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone has symmetric holdings and only rough beliefs about others’ chip preferences. The first set of proposals is likely exploratory: players test simple, non-extreme exchange rates that they think might be broadly acceptable. Because alligator and buffalo share identical values and rabbit’s values are similar, trades that make sense to the proposer will often also make sense to at least one responder. However, the three-player structure adds competition: an obviously profitable offer may attract multiple acceptances, so proposers might shade terms slightly in their own favor while keeping them acceptable. We should expect at least one trade to be executed this round, likely redistributing a small number of RED or BLUE chips toward whoever appears willing to pay in GREEN or BLUE.

After Round 0, holdings are slightly asymmetric. Alligator has traded away some GREEN for BLUE and then did not participate further, so he is modestly more BLUE-heavy and GREEN-poor. Buffalo has given up BLUE to Rabbit in exchange for RED, leaving her more RED-heavy and with fewer BLUE and GREEN. Rabbit has become more RED-heavy and slightly BLUE-poor relative to start. Everyone has observed that Rabbit happily accepted a slightly favorable RED-for-BLUE deal from Buffalo and that Alligator and Buffalo both declined Rabbit’s symmetric BLUE-for-GREEN trade, revealing that BLUE is not hugely more attractive than GREEN to them at a 1:1 rate. This information sets expectations: Rabbit is seen as an easygoing, calculative accepter of modest gains, Buffalo as fairness-oriented but open to roughly balanced surplus, and Alligator as seeking clear personal value. In Round 1, proposers can now target known preferences, especially trying to offer Rabbit trades that give her small but positive gains while improving their own mix of RED, BLUE, and GREEN. The main tension is between seeking extra RED (which everyone likes) and replenishing BLUE/GREEN stocks without making losing trades.

By Round 2, players have seen several small, mutually beneficial trades succeed and a couple of marginal trades in BLUE versus GREEN be rejected. The revealed pattern is that everyone will accept trades that clearly increase their own dollar value even by a modest amount, but they will reject neutral or slightly negative ones, especially in BLUE–GREEN swaps. Holdings are now more differentiated: Rabbit holds a relatively high number of RED and somewhat fewer BLUE; Buffalo holds a strong RED position with moderate BLUE and low GREEN; Alligator has regained some BLUE via GREEN trades and recently obtained more RED from Rabbit in exchange for BLUE. Because this is the final round, there is no reason to hold back: proposers should seek any last positive-surplus trades, even if they slightly favor the other side, as long as they personally gain. However, feasible gains are now thin: many of the easily exploitable differences have been consumed in earlier rounds. We should expect perhaps one more accepted trade that slightly rebalances holdings toward each player’s high-value colors, and one or two declined offers where proposers probe marginal BLUE–GREEN or RED–BLUE exchanges that responders correctly judge as not worthwhile.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game has heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start with 10 of each color. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, so they are a natural medium of exchange: nobody gains or loses in intrinsic value by swapping green for green, but they do lose from giving them away without getting enough private surplus back. The main gains from trade come from moving red chips away from the low-valuation player (bear, who values red at $0.50) toward the higher-valuation players (goose at $0.90 and buffalo at $0.80), and moving blue chips away from buffalo (who values blue at $0.20) toward goose and bear (who both value blue at $0.50). That creates scope for surplus-generating trades like buffalo selling blue to goose or bear for green or red, and bear selling red to goose or buffalo for green or blue. Because players only see their own values, they do not know ex ante that others value red or blue more or less than they do; instead, they infer others’ values from which directions of trade are proposed and accepted over time. Goose has a strong surplus motive to acquire red and is numerate enough to use the calculator effectively, but her ultimatum profile suggests she is tolerant of small personal gains and rarely rejects. Bear is more moderate and somewhat fairness-sensitive, with middling rejection rates and medium minimum acceptable payoffs. Buffalo is highly strategic and numerate, with low rejection rates and a strong willingness to exploit even small positive-sum opportunities. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), we expect modest but not fully efficient reallocation: some red shifts from bear to goose or buffalo, and some blue shifts from buffalo to the others. However, uncertainty about others’ valuations, randomization when multiple players accept, and the short horizon limit how far they can coordinate. Early in the game, players test the waters with somewhat conservative offers that guarantee themselves non-negative gains according to their calculator while trying not to give away too much. As they observe who accepts which directions of trade, they update beliefs and adjust proposals to become more targeted, especially in rounds 1 and 2. The simultaneous acceptances and random matching create a risk that a proposer cannot target their preferred partner, which slightly softens their offers (they want any acceptance to be profitable) and encourages recipients to accept when they see even modest positive surplus, since future opportunities are uncertain and their bargaining power per turn is limited.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, everyone still has symmetric endowments (10 of each color) and no information about others’ private chip values beyond general expectations. Each player uses this first round to probe the market: goose is likely to test whether others will part with red in exchange for green or blue; bear tests appetite for red in exchange for safer green; buffalo explores selling low-value blue in return for more valuable chips. Proposals are fairly conservative, aiming for clear positive gains on the proposer’s calculator, and recipients are inclined to accept any non-trivial positive surplus because there is little basis yet for strategic waiting or precise partner targeting.

By the start of round 1, observed trades have revealed partial information: others see that buffalo was willing to give up blue easily and accept red-for-green from bear, suggesting buffalo values red more than green and blue less than green. Goose and bear can infer that acquiring blue from buffalo is likely cheap, and that buffalo may pay something for red. Goose also just paid green for blue and then used blue to acquire red, so she is implicitly signaling a relatively high value on red. Players now start to refine their proposals: goose shifts more directly toward buying red, bear is more willing to part with red once there is clear demand, and buffalo tries to monetize blue while picking up more red or at least not losing too much value. Rejections are still rare when surplus is positive, but players are slightly more selective than in round 0.

Entering the final round, everyone knows the game is ending, so there is no reason to hold back surplus-improving trades. Goose has successfully increased her red holdings and has shown willingness to pay with both green and blue. Bear, after experimenting with selling red and blue for green, now understands that green is safe but doesn’t create extra private surplus and becomes more cautious about trading away too much value for negligible gains. Buffalo has learned that others are somewhat protective of green and increasingly selective about trades that hurt their calculators, so she looks for last-chance opportunities to sell blue or adjust her portfolio, but she avoids clearly negative deals. The combination of better information and the short horizon yields a mix of one or two additional small surplus trades and several rejected proposals that are too unfavorable to at least one recipient. Players no longer experiment with ambiguous offers; each proposal is aimed at obviously positive returns for the proposer, but recipients reject more sharply when gains are tiny or negative.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip-bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for all) and privately valued red/blue chips. Players know only their own red/blue valuations but can infer others’ incentives from observed trades. Across three rounds each player proposes once per round, with each proposal being a simple one-color-for-another-color trade that all non-senders can accept or reject simultaneously. If multiple accept, the recipient is chosen randomly, so proposers must design trades that are robustly profitable no matter which responder they get. The core strategic tension is that red and blue chips are privately valued and heterogeneous: Peacock values red highly ($0.80) and blue moderately ($0.50); Cat values red and blue equally ($0.50 each); Otter values red ($0.70) more than blue ($0.20). Green is a neutral store of value at $0.50 for everyone, so green trades are relatively transparent and safe. Proposers see their own net surplus for any candidate trade, and responders see their own surplus for any incoming trade, so mutually beneficial trades should often be accepted when they create clear positive-sum gains given the private values. Personality priors suggest all three are relatively low-rejection and cooperative, with Otter slightly more willing to punish unfairness. Given the valuations, the main positive-sum patterns are: (1) Otter should be willing to give up blue cheaply to gain red or green; (2) Peacock is willing to give blue to gain red or possibly green; (3) Cat is comparatively neutral and can act as a flexible middle player trading toward her moderate red and blue values and baseline green value. Over three rounds, players face uncertainty about others’ exact values and acceptance thresholds but will update beliefs from early trades. Because the horizon is short, they have limited scope for long-run reputation-building and instead aim to lock in clear gains quickly, especially using green as a safe benchmark. This forecast reconstructs one plausible complete game path where each player uses simple myopic surplus-improving logic plus mild fairness concerns.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 begins with symmetric inventories (10 of each color per player) and no direct information about others’ valuations beyond public ranges. Players are cautious but motivated to discover profitable directions. Given their priors and risk aversion, early trades are likely to involve green, which has clear common value, as an anchor. Each proposer will tentatively test whether others are willing to give up chips they probably value less (blue for Otter, red/blue for Cat) in exchange for green or for a chip the proposer values more. Responses will reveal initial hints about others’ relative preferences without large risks, since holdings are still balanced.

After Round 0, inventories diverge and players have some signals: Peacock was willing to give up blue for green; Cat accepted that, indicating she doesn’t strongly prefer green to blue. Cat then traded blue for red with Otter, revealing that Otter is eager to acquire red and is willing to part with blue at a discount. Otter’s declined blue-for-green offer was unappealing to both Peacock and Cat, reinforcing that blue is not especially attractive to them at the offered rate. Entering Round 1, players increasingly see blue as Otter’s low-value chip and red as Otter’s target asset, while Peacock still values red most and is comfortable holding green. Cat has upgraded her red holdings and may now look for modest, low-risk value tweaks using green as a benchmark.

By Round 2, role patterns have sharpened: Otter consistently tries to offload blue to gain higher-value chips, signaling blue is cheap for her; Peacock and Cat have both been willing to trade away blue for better-valued assets and to trade modestly around green. After earlier accepted trades, Peacock now holds more red and green and fewer blue, aligning with her preferences; Cat holds more red and green and somewhat fewer blue, close to her neutral stance; Otter holds excess blue and relatively fewer red and green. With only one round remaining, players prioritize clear gains over further experimentation. Peacock is still happy to convert residual blue into green. Cat, relatively content, may make a small, nearly neutral trade if it seems fair. Otter, facing a short horizon and many low-value blue chips, makes a more aggressive final attempt to swap blue for red, but the others, now understanding blue’s low relative value and already near favorable portfolios, reject anything that would lower their own payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation:

Badger highly values both red and blue at $1.00, squid values blue a lot ($1.00) and red very little ($0.10), and camel has intermediate valuations: blue $0.80, red $0.40. This creates strong gains-from-trade: squid is eager to dump red for blue or green; badger wants both red and blue and is relatively indifferent between them; camel is willing to trade red for blue or green if terms are good. Uncertainty is purely about others’ valuations—each sees only their own—but the public value ranges ($0.10–$1.00 for red/blue, fixed $0.50 for green) provide coarse bounds, and the on-screen surplus calculator lets all players react perfectly to the profitability of any posted trade for themselves. Strategically, rational players should never accept a negative-surplus trade. However, because each proposer only sees their own payoff calculator, proposals may initially be myopically skewed toward their own advantage and test the waters. Over three rounds, proposers will likely converge on mutually beneficial, somewhat lopsided trades that exploit value differences: (1) squid trading away red for blue (ideally from badger, second-best from camel); (2) camel trading red for blue or green, especially when the calculator shows even small gains; (3) badger converting green or occasionally blue into red while maintaining or increasing total value. Ultimatum-task profiles imply: squid is easygoing and acceptance-tolerant, badger is moderately demanding, and camel is very tolerant of small gains—so camel will accept thin-margin positive trades, while badger needs more clearly positive offers. Random resolution among multiple acceptors adds a coordination externality: proposers that post very attractive offers risk giving value away to the less preferred counterparty. Given the short horizon (three rounds) and full surplus visibility, we can expect a few early probing offers, followed by at least one clearly efficient trade where low-value chips move toward those who value them more, boosting at least two players’ payoffs without making anyone worse off.

Below is a forecast of a full playthrough consistent with these incentives and personas. All decisions respect individual surplus calculations and the simultaneous-response rule, with players acting as self-interested but not fully omniscient reasoners.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one has yet inferred others’ valuations. Squid knows red is nearly worthless to him and blue is most valuable; he will likely test a red-for-blue or red-for-green trade that looks good for him and hope someone bites. Badger, valuing both red and blue equally at $1.00, is happy to acquire either at the expense of green if terms are mildly favorable, but will avoid obvious losses. Camel, who is easygoing and tolerant of small advantages to others, will accept almost any strictly positive-surplus deal, especially those that trade some red for blue or green. Round 0 will mostly feature exploratory offers revealing how aggressive proposers are and which counter-parties are more willing to accept modest gains.

After round 0 trades, players update their beliefs slightly. Squid has successfully unloaded red to both camel (directly) and indirectly strengthened camel’s red position via badger’s trade; squid has also revealed a willingness to accept modest but positive gains. Camel has accepted two different trades and signaled he is willing to take small but positive surplus, especially swapping red for blue or green. Badger has demonstrated a preference to convert green into red at favorable rates. Inventories are now more differentiated: squid holds fewer red and more blue; camel holds more red, fewer blue and green; badger holds more red and fewer green. In round 1, squid will likely attempt a more targeted value-enhancing trade (e.g., offering some blue—which others value highly—for a larger amount of green, which is mid-value to everyone). Badger, with many red chips, will continue seeking red at the expense of green but must keep trades at least slightly profitable given his medium rejection threshold. Camel, flush with red but low on blue and green, will try to rebalance by converting some red into blue or green on thin margins, expecting others to accept clearly positive deals for themselves.

By round 2, each player has observed several profitable exchanges. Patterns are clear: squid systematically dumps red and now also uses some blue to buy green when the rate favors him; badger uses green and occasionally blue to build an even larger red pile; camel repeatedly sells red for blue or green on even or slightly favorable terms. All have learned that the others reject negative-surplus trades and accept clearly positive ones. Inventories are now skewed: squid is light on red and relatively heavy on blue and green; badger is red-heavy and green-poor with a moderate blue position; camel is still red-heavy but has rebuilt some blue and green. Proposals in round 1 become more targeted: gorilla explores using her extra blue; llama carefully tests whether either partner will give up blue; whale again tries to convert blue into more valuable colors but adjusts generosity to induce acceptance.

By round 2, clear patterns have emerged. Gorilla’s earlier trades show she values red more than blue and is willing to part with some blue to gain red, though not at a steep loss. Llama has shown willingness to spend green for blue and to accept blue for red when the terms aren’t painful, consistent with a strong preference for blue. Whale has consistently tried to trade blue away for green or red and has accepted relatively small but positive gains. Everyone now has better beliefs: gorilla sees whale as a natural counterparty for red/green vs. blue trades; llama understands that whale undervalues blue; whale sees that both partners like blue more than he does. With only three proposal turns left, players look for any remaining sure-positive opportunities, even if small. The trades in this round are more incremental and efficiency-seeking, aiming to tilt chips a bit further toward each player’s high-value colors before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 each. RED and BLUE differ by player: duck values RED and BLUE at $1, lion values RED at $0.90 and BLUE at $0.50, and tiger values RED at $1 and BLUE at $0.30. So RED is valuable to all, especially duck and tiger; BLUE is most valuable only to duck. GREEN is a stable medium-good for everyone. Efficient trades generally move BLUE toward duck (who values it most) and somewhat away from tiger (who values it least), and RED toward tiger and duck (who value it at $1) and away from lion (who values it at $0.90). Because each proposer sees their own surplus calculator and responders see theirs, everyone can instantly spot whether any given trade is privately profitable. However, they do not know others’ values, only that RED and BLUE are somewhere between $0.10–$1.00 and GREEN is fixed at $0.50. This creates strategic uncertainty: a proposer doesn’t know how good their offer is for each recipient, and responders must infer motives from terms. Duck’s persona suggests cooperative, low-conflict behavior, willing to accept small positive gains and to propose fair-looking trades. Lion and tiger have higher minimum acceptable thresholds from ultimatum behavior and moderate rejection tendencies, so they’re more willing to pass on low-margin or slightly disadvantageous trades. Over only three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, players are unlikely to converge on exact valuations, so trades will tend to be conservative: clearly positive for the proposer and at least modestly positive for one recipient. Random tie-breaking when multiple players accept means proposers must think about how attractive their offer might be to both potential recipients, since they cannot pick which partner executes. This forecast assumes players are cautious but responsive to clear dollar gains, and that they avoid trades that generate a visible loss on the calculator.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 of each chip type and only knows their own RED/BLUE values plus the common GREEN value. Proposers will likely start with relatively symmetric, low-stakes trades to test the water and gauge what others might accept. Duck, being cooperative, may offer clearly beneficial deals to encourage trading. Lion and tiger, with higher minimum acceptable thresholds, may propose trades that slightly favor themselves while testing demand for RED and BLUE. Responders will lean on the surplus calculator to avoid losses and might accept modest but not tiny positive gains.

By the second round, some information has leaked: lion knows duck was eager to trade GREEN for BLUE, and both lion and duck see that tiger tried to buy RED using BLUE. This suggests BLUE may be relatively less valuable to tiger, while RED is relatively more valuable. Duck’s acceptance of tiger’s BLUE-for-RED trade suggests duck values BLUE highly and is flexible with RED. Players will now adjust offers slightly, with duck aiming to keep exploiting BLUE gains, lion trying to improve their RED holdings without overpaying, and tiger trying to further convert BLUE into RED where profitable.

In the final round, players have only one proposal left each, so they will look for any remaining clearly positive trades. Duck has consistently profited by swapping GREEN and RED for BLUE, suggesting a final attempt to buy BLUE from whoever still holds it. Lion has failed to close a deal so far and may remain cautious, not wanting to risk a visible loss. Tiger has been successfully converting BLUE into RED with duck and may try one more small, safe RED-for-BLUE trade at the end. With no future rounds, there is no reason to hold back chips for future bargaining; only immediate net value gains matter.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game revolves around exchanging red and blue chips, which have heterogeneous private values across players, while green chips are common-value and therefore safe to trade at par. Every player starts with 10 of each color. Bear values blue chips highly ($1.00), is almost indifferent or low on red ($0.20), and sees green at $0.50. Goose and Ram value both red and blue at $0.80 and green at $0.50. This means blue is strictly the most valuable color for Bear and tied for most valuable for Goose and Ram, while red is much less valuable for Bear than for Goose and Ram. The main efficient pattern is for Bear to trade away red chips to Goose/Ram in exchange for blue chips, because a red chip is worth $0.20 to Bear but $0.80 to Goose/Ram, while a blue chip is worth $1.00 to Bear and $0.80 to Goose/Ram. Each such trade increases total surplus. By contrast, trades that move green chips do not change total surplus and are effectively just redistributing a common-value asset, which may still be used as a medium of exchange or small compromise, but there is no pure gain from trade. Strategic uncertainty arises because players only see their own values, not others’. Bear will know red is bad for him and blue is good, but not that Goose and Ram also like red, nor that they value blue less. Goose and Ram see that red and blue are symmetric for themselves, so they may treat Bear’s early requests for blue and offers of red as weak signals that Bear values blue more and red less. All players have an on-screen surplus calculator, so they will not accept trades that are privately losing. Persona cues suggest Bear is relatively generous and tolerant of low payoffs, while Goose and especially Ram are quite willing to accept even small positive gains and rarely reject. Over just three rounds with one proposal per player per round, the likely dynamics are: (1) Bear experiments early with red-for-blue offers aimed at Goose and/or Ram, trying to secure blue at a modest exchange rate; (2) Goose is willing to accept nearly any positive surplus deal and may propose moderate trades that look fair to her own calculator; (3) Ram, who is numerate and strategically minded, is quick to accept beneficial trades and may also propose slightly more self-favoring but still acceptable exchanges. Because acceptance choices are simultaneous and the recipient is random if multiple accept, proposers face some uncertainty about which partner they will actually trade with. However, given symmetry between Goose and Ram, Bear’s optimal surplus-improving proposals look similar regardless of which of them accepts. We should therefore expect at least one or two accepted trades where Bear gives up red for blue, modest green-side or symmetric trades proposed by Goose/Ram, and possibly one or more declined proposals when a proposer overreaches or misjudges how attractive a given chip exchange is to the others.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have 10 of each chip color. No one knows others’ private values; they only know their own and the public ranges. Bear sees that blue is very valuable and red is low-value, so an early strategy is to offer red for blue, likely in a conservative ratio to ensure someone’s calculator shows a gain. Goose and Ram see red and blue as equally valuable, so their acceptance thresholds for small trades will be very low as long as the trade is non-negative for them. This first round is thus a feeling-out phase: Bear sets a tone with an initial red-for-blue or red-for-green style offer; Goose and Ram respond based on their calculators; subsequent proposers (Goose and Ram later in the round) update beliefs from any observed acceptance/decline and may keep proposals small to avoid visible losses.

Going into round 1, Bear has successfully converted some red to blue with Ram, revealing to Goose and Ram that Bear is eager to acquire blue and shed red. Goose’s first proposal was declined, suggesting that offering green for blue at 1:1 was unattractive to both others’ calculators—both Bear and Ram would see that swapping a $1.00 or $0.80 blue chip for a $0.50 green chip is a clear loss. Ram’s small red-for-green trade was accepted by Goose, showing her willingness to accept even tiny positive gains. With this new information, Bear can be more confident that modestly favorable red-for-blue ratios will continue to be accepted, especially by Ram, who has already made one trade. Goose now understands that she must either improve terms or target red instead of blue, while Ram understands that both Bear and Goose are open to small positive-sum trades.

By round 2, several trades have executed. Bear has traded away a substantial number of low-value red chips for high-value blue chips, improving his portfolio. Goose has participated in at least one accepted trade with Ram and one with Bear, learning that both partners will accept small but strictly positive gains and that green is less attractive to them than red or blue. Ram has engaged in both red-for-green and red-for-blue directions, observing that Bear’s and Goose’s behaviors are consistent with maximizing own-value via the surplus calculator. With only one proposal left for each player, they will either attempt one more incremental improvement or refrain from overly aggressive terms that risk being declined. Bear is likely to continue his pattern of modestly favorable red-for-blue offers. Goose may try a safe small trade involving red, and Ram, being strategic and numerate, may only propose if he sees a clearly positive expected gain given observed responses so far.
