parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric inventories (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN), but they value colors differently. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so no mutually beneficial trades involving only GREEN vs. GREEN or trivial reshuffling exist; GREEN only matters as a medium of exchange if someone is temporarily willing to give it up to get a more valuable private color. The real surplus comes from trading BLUE and RED, which are privately valued.

Private values:
- lion-blue-472 (Lion): BLUE=$0.60, RED=$0.50, GREEN=$0.50 → BLUE > RED=GREEN.
- octopus-green-4361 (Octopus): BLUE=$0.10, RED=$0.20, GREEN=$0.50 → GREEN > RED > BLUE.
- ram-purple-2824 (Ram): BLUE=$1.00, RED=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50 → BLUE >> GREEN > RED.

Thus, Ram has very strong demand for BLUE and is comparatively happy to give up RED. Octopus is unusually attached to GREEN and somewhat prefers RED to BLUE. Lion likes BLUE best but is fairly indifferent between RED and GREEN. Because everyone only knows their own values, they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances.

Incentives:
- Ram: wants to acquire BLUE (especially from Lion, who may undervalue BLUE relative to Ram) by paying in RED or possibly GREEN. Ram will be stingy (low social preference) but rational, looking for clear private surplus; given their ultimatum prior, they’ll make tough but not outrageously unfair offers and accept anything that increases their value.
- Lion: moderate pro-social orientation but still surplus-focused. Lion likes BLUE but is not massively skewed; she may sell some BLUE if she can pick up extra RED/GREEN cheaply, though trades where she gives up BLUE are risky. Being numerate and low-rejection in ultimatum, she will accept any strictly positive gain and propose trades that show a visible surplus in the calculator.
- Octopus: strongly values GREEN and is the only player who sees BLUE as almost worthless. Octopus has a natural role as a GREEN seller or buyer depending on perceived prices but will be quite reluctant to give up too much GREEN. Their cooperative orientation and high numeracy mean they’ll be willing to accept modest but positive gains, and they may propose relatively fair-looking splits.

Strategic uncertainty:
- Players don’t know each other’s valuations, so first-round offers are exploratory. Most will test small, conservative trades (1–3 chips) to avoid large mistakes and to read others’ willingness to pay. The random recipient rule when multiple opponents accept weakens targeted bargaining power: you can’t perfectly choose your partner, so you might shade offers to be attractive to at least one counterpart without being overly generous.
- Because GREEN is common-value and mid-range for everyone, offering GREEN for RED or BLUE can look roughly fair on face value. However, privately, Octopus thinks GREEN is extremely valuable and will resist giving it away unless they get a measurable surplus.

Across three rounds, each player gets 3 chances to propose (9 turns total). With only three rounds, there isn’t time for complex reputation building; instead, you expect: (i) early probing trades, some declined due to misaligned prices, and (ii) at least a couple of accepted trades later, especially involving Ram buying BLUE with RED and/or GREEN. All three have low rejection tendencies from ultimatum, so any clearly positive-sum offer on their calculator is likely to be accepted. Net result: modest efficiency gains mainly from reallocating BLUE from Octopus to Ram and possibly some RED reshuffling between Ram and Octopus, with Lion making a mix of exploratory and protective offers.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, everyone is essentially in information-gathering mode. No one yet knows who values which colors, so they will likely propose small, seemingly reasonable trades that don’t risk large losses. The proposals will focus on colors that each sender is comparatively willing to give up, aiming to see if anyone bites at mild exchange rates. Because all players have low ultimatum rejection tendencies, even modest gains should trigger acceptance, but mispricing due to ignorance can still cause declines.

By round 1, some information has already surfaced: Lion sees Ram accepting to trade for BLUE and to give up RED, hinting Ram values BLUE highly and RED relatively less. Octopus sees Ram eagerly trading BLUE against RED, confirming Ram’s BLUE hunger. Lion has also revealed a willingness to trade RED for GREEN at roughly even terms, which may signal indifference between them. Players will adjust: Ram will double down on acquiring BLUE; Lion will be more cautious about giving up BLUE but may offload excess RED; Octopus, having shed some BLUE and gained RED, may seek to monetize RED or cautiously trade GREEN if the surplus is clear.

Round 2 is the final opportunity, so players will weigh marginal gains carefully. Inventories have shifted: Ram has accumulated much more BLUE and parted with significant RED (and later some GREEN); Lion has taken in some RED and GREEN but also given up BLUE; Octopus has offloaded BLUE for RED and seen others’ willingness to trade. With time running out, small, clearly positive trades are most likely: Ram may still grab last bits of underpriced BLUE, and Lion or Octopus may attempt one more modest reshuffle if they see a safe surplus. However, some proposals will be declined because at the margin they no longer move anyone’s value meaningfully, or they look like thinly veiled attempts to exploit private information.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, whose values differ by player, while green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each chip type, and there are only three rounds with one proposal turn per player each round. Because only one chip type can be requested and one different chip type can be offered per proposal, trades are simple one-for-one (or many-for-many) single-color swaps, but the strategic environment is richer: players know their own values exactly and see a surplus calculator for any draft they consider, yet they do not know the others’ private values for red and blue. This creates uncertainty about why others accept or reject particular terms and about which color is relatively more valuable to each counterpart. Buffalo values RED highly ($0.90) and BLUE minimally ($0.10), with GREEN at $0.50. Cat has a modest preference for BLUE over RED (BLUE=$0.20, RED=$0.40, GREEN=$0.50), whereas Duck strongly prefers BLUE ($0.90) and regards RED as low ($0.40), with GREEN again common at $0.50. From a social surplus standpoint, the efficient pattern is for RED chips to flow toward Buffalo and BLUE chips to flow toward Duck, with GREEN serving as a neutral medium: nobody gains or loses intrinsic value from exchanging GREEN among players, but green can help signal fairness or sweeten deals. However, given the short horizon of only three rounds and the random resolution when multiple players accept, proposers may pursue fairly conservative trades that guarantee them small positive gains, and responders may accept any offer that yields even a small improvement, especially Duck, whose prior indicates an extremely low rejection threshold in ultimatum settings. Buffalo, with high empathy and cooperation and low ultimatum threshold, is likely to propose seemingly “fair” trades that still exploit their high value for RED and low value for BLUE, potentially offering BLUE or even some GREEN to obtain RED. Cat, with a medium minimum acceptable share, may be more selective: she will likely accept modest positive gains but may avoid trades that feel lopsided even if slightly positive in value. Duck, with strong numeracy and almost no history of rejecting in ultimatum tasks, is likely to opportunistically accept almost any strictly positive gain and design proposals that exploit his very high BLUE value by selling RED or even GREEN to acquire BLUE cheaply. Over three rounds, we should expect some initial experimentation to discover others’ likely valuations (e.g., Buffalo testing whether someone will trade RED for BLUE plus a Green kicker) followed by more targeted proposals that steer RED to Buffalo and BLUE to Duck once patterns of acceptance become clearer. Because acceptances are simultaneous and random among acceptors, proposers may sometimes craft “globally attractive” deals hoping either potential counterpart will accept, but this also adds risk: they cannot target the counterpart whose valuation they have best inferred. The forecast below reconstructs a plausible full three-round sequence where players generally avoid value-destroying trades, make locally profitable deals when clear, and adjust proposals as they infer that Buffalo covets RED and Duck covets BLUE, with Cat sometimes acting as an intermediary to move chips in those directions while maintaining her own surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one knows the others’ exact red/blue valuations, so proposals are exploratory and relatively conservative. Buffalo, valuing RED highly and BLUE very low, is motivated to use low-value BLUE (or neutral GREEN) to buy RED, but she may start with a small, fair-appearing offer to avoid rejection. Cat is still gauging whether others prefer RED or BLUE, and her medium fairness threshold means she wants at least modest gains. Duck is highly tolerant of unequal splits and numerate, so he will quickly accept any clearly positive trade, especially ones that give him BLUE for chips he values less, and he may propose an aggressive BLUE-acquiring trade of his own once he sees others’ willingness to move BLUE or RED.

By round 1, players have some evidence: Duck’s acceptance of Buffalo’s RED-for-BLUE trade suggests he may not value RED especially highly relative to BLUE, and both Cat and Buffalo saw that Cat’s attempt to buy BLUE with GREEN drew no interest, suggesting that neither counterpart is eager to give up BLUE for neutral GREEN at small margins. Duck’s failed attempt to swap RED for BLUE at a 1:1 ratio with no takers also hints that others view BLUE as at least as valuable as RED, if not more so. Buffalo continues trying to amass RED while offloading BLUE, but now she may be slightly more cautious with terms. Cat, having just had a proposal rejected, may pivot and try selling RED (which she values moderately) for GREEN or BLUE. Duck, seeing that his previous equal swap was unattractive, might now either offer a discount (more RED for fewer BLUE) or start using GREEN as a sweetener to entice someone to give up BLUE.

Going into the final round, behavior has revealed strong patterns: Buffalo has successfully purchased RED twice at terms that indicate she values RED more than GREEN and BLUE, and the others have been willing to part with RED when compensated with GREEN, BLUE, or both. Cat has managed to trade RED for BLUE, showing her mild preference for BLUE at favorable rates, while Duck’s acceptance of RED-for-BLUE trades at generous margins confirms that BLUE is indeed his highest-value chip. Everyone now faces the final chance to adjust portfolios. Buffalo tries to make one last push to convert low-value BLUE into high-value RED. Cat, having already improved her BLUE holdings, now looks for any final small surplus—likely by moving chips she values relatively less (RED) in exchange for GREEN or BLUE if the deal is clearly beneficial. Duck, highly tolerant of small gains and well aware that BLUE is his key asset, may attempt an aggressive swap to grab more BLUE using RED or GREEN as bait, but other players, now more certain that BLUE is highly valued, will only accept if the surplus is clearly positive for them.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values on red and blue chips plus common-value green chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Greens are worth $0.50 to all players, so any trade that destroys total greens tends to destroy surplus; players will rarely give up many greens unless they think the other colors are much more or less valuable to them than to others. Red and blue values differ sharply: butterfly values RED at $0.30 and BLUE at $0.10; kangaroo values RED at $0.70 and BLUE at $0.20; squid values RED at $0.40 and BLUE at $0.80. So squid loves blue and somewhat likes red; kangaroo loves red and modestly likes blue; butterfly prefers red over blue but is lowest-value overall. Because each participant only knows their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from observed behavior. However, the surplus calculator shown on each proposal lets every player see exactly their own profit or loss from any posted trade, so acceptance behavior will be nearly perfectly individually rational: they will almost never accept strictly negative-value trades. Since multiple players can accept and the recipient is then chosen at random, proposers face partner-selection risk. They can design offers that favor the partner they most want to trade with, but they cannot directly choose that partner. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), a typical path is: (1) early-round exploration with relatively conservative, near-fair offers as players test how others respond, (2) mid-game attempts to exploit perceived comparative advantages (squid trying to buy blue cheaply; kangaroo trying to buy red cheaply), and (3) late-round, more aggressive or desperate offers if players feel they are behind or if they misread others’ valuations. The personas imply butterfly is cooperative and tolerant of small or even zero gains, kangaroo is more hard-nosed and self-focused, and squid is more fairness-sensitive but also strategic. That mix suggests squid will avoid exploitative-seeming offers and may reject offers that look unfair even when mildly profitable, whereas butterfly accepts many small-gain deals and kangaroo pushes for high personal margins. Overall, we should see a few mutually beneficial red–blue trades that move chips toward their highest-value holders, some cautious green usage (e.g., kangaroo or squid using small amounts of green to pay for especially valuable colors), and a handful of declined offers when the proposer misjudges how valuable their own chips are relative to others’.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone has symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but only knows their own payoffs. No trades have yet revealed information, so each player is guessing about how much others value red vs. blue. Butterfly is cooperative and may start with a relatively fair, modest proposal to test responsiveness. Kangaroo is self-focused and will likely reach for a high-margin red-buying trade. Squid, who values blue a lot and has medium fairness standards, will be interested in buying blue and selling red, but will also be wary of looking or feeling exploited. This opening round is mostly exploratory, with each player calibrating what others will accept while avoiding obvious losses.

Going into Round 1, two trades have executed. Trade 0 (butterfly buying blue with green from squid) signals that squid is willing to give up some blue for green, hinting that squid values green at least as much as blue on the margin, or that they are not yet fully strategic. Trade 1 (kangaroo buying red with green from butterfly) reveals that kangaroo wants red and will pay green for it, implying red is quite valuable to kangaroo. Butterfly has been flexible and accepted a modest-gain trade, consistent with their cooperative persona. Current holdings: butterfly now has slightly more red and fewer green, squid has fewer blue and more green, kangaroo has more red and fewer green. This information nudges players to infer that kangaroo’s red valuation is high and squid’s blue valuation is high, making red-for-blue and blue-for-red trades natural focal points. Players may start crafting more targeted proposals aimed at these perceived tastes, but still under significant uncertainty about precise values.

By Round 2, squid has managed to acquire some extra blue and give up a bit of green, kangaroo has increased red and regained some blue at the cost of green, and butterfly has slightly shifted toward red while spending green. The pattern suggests that kangaroo and squid are both leaning into their high-value colors (red for kangaroo, blue for squid), and butterfly is playing a more adaptive, low-threshold role. With only one proposal left per player, each now faces end-game incentives: there is no future need to preserve reputation or information, so proposals can be more aggressive. However, each responder still sees exact personal payoffs from the calculator and will refuse strict losses. We should see either modest, mutually beneficial trades that tighten each player’s portfolio toward their favorites, or ambitious but overly one-sided offers that get declined. Given the personas, butterfly may make one last cooperative but self-beneficial attempt; kangaroo may try a somewhat greedy red-acquisition proposal that fails; squid is most likely to craft a fair-ish, surplus-creating trade, but might still be declined if it relies on green in a way others dislike.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values on red and blue chips, with green chips known to be worth $0.50 to everyone. Fox and Frog value red chips relatively highly ($0.60 and $0.70), while Bat values red very little ($0.20). Conversely, Fox values blue the most ($1.00), Bat moderately ($0.60), and Frog the least ($0.30). Since green is common-value and intermediate ($0.50), it serves as a safe, mutually understandable medium. The main surplus opportunities lie in moving red chips away from Bat toward Fox or Frog, and moving blue chips away from Frog toward Fox or Bat. Because each player only knows their own payoffs, they must infer others’ preferences from the proposals observed. All three participants have low minimum acceptable thresholds and low rejection rates in prior ultimatum tasks, suggesting they are inclined to accept even modest positive gains and to make reasonably cooperative offers. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat fair-looking in chip counts, testing which directions of trade attract interest. Over the three rounds, as histories accumulate, players can adjust offers—slightly improving terms when earlier attempts fail, or leveraging green chips as a compromise asset when uncertainty about red/blue values remains. However, strategic complexity is constrained by the short horizon (three rounds) and the rule that each proposal can only involve one color bought and one color sold at a time. Since acceptances are decided privately and, in case of multiple acceptances, matched randomly, senders must design offers that are individually attractive to at least one counterpart, while being robust to either counterpart ending up as the actual trading partner. The likely pattern is modest, value-increasing trades rather than extreme exploitation, with some mutually beneficial reallocation of red and blue toward their higher-value holders and green used sparingly to bridge modest valuation gaps.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but have no direct information about others’ private values. Given their cooperative and low-rejection priors, opening offers are likely to be modest and not too demanding. Players will test simple red-for-blue or red/blue-for-green exchanges that are clearly beneficial from their own perspective while hoping they are at least acceptable to someone else. Because no one yet knows who values what, these early offers prioritize learning and securing small gains rather than pushing aggressively for maximum surplus.

After Round 0, some structure in holdings and inferred preferences has emerged. Bat has traded away several low-value red chips in exchange for blue, revealing that red may be relatively cheap for her and that she is open to giving generous red-for-blue terms. Fox, having accepted red for blue earlier and then seen a red-for-green offer declined, may infer that at least one counterpart does not like trading away red for green at an even rate. Frog has executed a blue-for-red trade with Bat, suggesting he values red more and is willing to part with some blue when the terms are favorable. In Round 1, proposals are likely to be slightly more targeted: Bat continues to unload red for assets she suspects others value; Fox experiments with smaller or more balanced trades after a declined offer; Frog refines blue-for-red or blue-for-green trades that align better with inferred counterpart preferences.

By Round 2, each player has seen multiple accepted trades and at least one declined proposal, giving them a clearer picture of relative valuations. Bat appears comfortable shedding red for blue and occasionally for other colors, confirming to others that red is relatively cheap for her. Fox has shown willingness to acquire red and to trade red or green when favorable, and has just completed a green-for-red trade with Frog, strengthening the impression that Fox values red strongly but is flexible with green. Frog has repeatedly moved blue out in exchange for red and, more recently, green, consistent with blue being his least-valued color. In this final round, with limited time left, players focus on straightforward, obviously beneficial swaps rather than experimentation: Bat tries one more profitable red-for-blue trade, Fox fine-tunes a trade that balances her red and blue to near-preferred levels, and Frog offers a conservative blue-for-green or blue-for-red exchange to squeeze out remaining surplus without risking declines.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features two symmetric high-blue/low-red players (dog-green-8079 and turtle-red-9966) and one relatively red-oriented player (parrot-pink-5400). Everyone values green at the same $0.50, so green is a neutral common-value chip that can be used as a medium of exchange. Dog and Turtle both value blue at $1 and red at $0.50, while Parrot values red at $0.60 and blue at $0.30, making Parrot the natural red buyer and blue seller from the perspective of Dog and Turtle. However, players only know their own values, so they face uncertainty about others’ preferences and must infer them from observed trades. The surplus calculator makes each trade’s personal gain or loss transparent at the moment of decision, so people are unlikely to accept strictly negative trades, but they might accept small-gain or roughly break-even trades for strategic or fairness reasons. Dog’s ultimatum behavior suggests tolerance of low payoffs and little inclination to punish, so he may accept small advantages and propose simple low-risk trades. Parrot, with medium generosity and very low rejection rates, is likely to offer relatively fair, mutually beneficial trades once patterns emerge. Turtle shows medium minimum acceptable and higher rejection rates, suggesting more selectivity and a willingness to reject modest or ambiguous gains, especially when she suspects she is giving up valuable blue. Over only three rounds (nine total proposals), participants have limited time to explore, infer others’ values, and rearrange inventories, so we should expect modest but positive surplus creation, especially via trades where Dog or Turtle provide red to Parrot in exchange for blue or, secondarily, green, and occasional declined offers when Turtle feels the trade is too one-sided or when others are unsure about counterpart valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 of each chip color and know only their own values. Dog and Turtle each see blue as highly valuable and red as low, while Parrot sees red as relatively better and blue as weak. None of them know this structure yet. In round 0, initial offers are likely to be exploratory and conservative, with players testing small-quantity trades that look good given their own values and hoping someone finds them acceptable. Because everyone still has symmetric inventories, proposers cannot infer much about others’ types from holdings, so early behavior focuses on generating any positive surplus without taking big inventory risks. Given Dog’s low rejection threshold and Parrot’s low rejection behavior, early accepted trades are most likely to occur when one side clearly gets a modest gain and the other side is at least not losing by their own calculation. Turtle, being more selective, may start more cautiously and decline trades that feel extractive or heavily skewed against her perceived value of blue.

By the start of round 1, Dog has traded some red for blue with Parrot, and Parrot has moved some blue to Turtle in exchange for red. These early trades reveal partial structure: Parrot has shown willingness to part with blue for red to both Dog and Turtle, which strongly suggests to them that Parrot values blue less and red more than they do. Turtle, having just accepted a mildly favorable blue-for-red trade, may feel she captured value but also become wary of giving up too much red later. Dog now holds relatively more blue and less red, reinforcing his role as a blue-rich, red-poor player. Strategically, Dog can try to leverage Parrot’s apparent preference for red to convert more low-value red into high-value blue, while Parrot may attempt to balance being fair with both partners and avoid appearing exploitative. Turtle’s higher rejection propensity means she may now demand more favorable terms and reject offers that look small or ambiguous, especially if they reduce her blue inventory.

Entering the final round, patterns are clearer: Parrot has consistently been willing to trade away blue for red or green, while Dog and Turtle tend to seek blue or green in exchange for red. Dog’s inventory now tilts further toward high-value blue and neutral green, while his red stock is lower, meaning marginal red is increasingly scarce for him. Parrot, in contrast, holds more red and somewhat fewer blue chips, matching her values but limiting her ability to profit from further blue-for-red trades. Turtle has accumulated some extra blue and green through earlier exchanges but is conscious of the approaching end of the game and may be stricter about any trade that does not clearly increase her dollar payoff. With only three proposal turns left, each proposer faces a deadline effect: they will propose trades that yield clear, immediate surplus according to their calculator, and responders will be less inclined to experiment. This likely produces one or two more small but positive-sum trades, especially involving Dog or Turtle giving up low-value red for moderate quantities of blue or green from Parrot, and some final declinations when the remaining inventory combinations no longer support strong mutual gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip inventories (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) and know only their own payoff values. GREEN is common-value at $0.50, so everyone privately sees GREEN as the most valuable color relative to BLUE ($0.10) and, for two players, also relative to RED ($0.30). UNICORN has a strictly higher value for RED ($0.50) than the others ($0.30), so efficient surplus comes from trades where UNICORN gives up GREEN (or BLUE) to accumulate RED, while BADGER and PEACOCK move in the opposite direction if they discover this pattern. However, because each player only sees their own values, no one initially knows that UNICORN values RED more than they do. Proposers see an on-screen surplus calculator that shows their own net change for any candidate trade, so they will only propose deals that are strictly beneficial to themselves. Responders likewise only accept offers that generate non-negative surplus for them. Given personas, BADGER is cooperative and tolerant of small inequities, PEACOCK is fair-minded and strategically sharp, and UNICORN is willing to accept almost anything that is not obviously harmful but tends to be self-focused as a proposer. The main source of uncertainty is who values which color most and how aggressively others will chase GREEN vs RED. Across three short rounds, players experiment with small, positive-surplus trades that look nearly value-neutral from the responder’s perspective, gradually inferring that GREEN is uniformly high-value and that some players might prize RED more. Because BLUE is low-value for everyone, it is a natural medium of exchange and is often used as the offered color to buy either GREEN or RED. Randomization when multiple responders accept makes it risky to float overly generous offers that many might jump on; thus proposers tend to structure trades so that only one counterpart (the likely target) has clear positive gains, nudging the equilibrium toward personalized, mildly asymmetric deals rather than broad, competitive bidding.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any trading yet, so proposals are exploratory and conservative. All players know GREEN is valuable, but only UNICORN knows that RED is also worth $0.50 to them. Because they cannot see others’ valuations, BADGER and PEACOCK will likely assume that GREEN is the main prize and that RED/BLUE are relatively low-value. Proposals therefore tend to offer BLUE or RED to acquire GREEN, testing whether others are willing to part with GREEN at modest rates. Responders are cautious but open to small gains; given UNICORN’s low rejection threshold, UNICORN is especially likely to accept any non-harmful trade, while PEACOCK will be more selective and BADGER somewhat forgiving but still value-protective.

After round 0, the only executed trade was UNICORN swapping 1 BLUE for 1 RED with BADGER. This public history subtly signals that UNICORN may like RED more than BLUE, but it is still ambiguous because the deal was value-neutral for BADGER and (from BADGER’s perspective) might just reflect simple diversification. Inventories now show UNICORN slightly heavier in RED and lighter in BLUE, while BADGER is the reverse. Players have not yet tested GREEN in an accepted trade, so GREEN retains its special status as a chip few are willing to surrender cheaply. In round 1, proposers are more focused: UNICORN tries again to accumulate RED using BLUE, while BADGER and PEACOCK become slightly more suspicious of one-for-one RED trades and lean more toward offers that look fair in their own calculators, especially ones involving BLUE as the giveaway color.

By the start of round 2, two accepted trades have occurred, both initiated by UNICORN, each exchanging BLUE for RED. Publicly, UNICORN’s inventory now shows a meaningful tilt toward RED and away from BLUE; BADGER and PEACOCK each gave up a bit of RED to UNICORN in return for low-value BLUE. Even though BADGER and PEACOCK do not know exact values, they can infer that UNICORN likely places relatively high value on RED or low value on BLUE—or both. That makes them more reluctant to trade away RED at slight or zero apparent gain. GREEN has still not been traded successfully, reinforcing its status as a protected, high-value asset. In this last round, BADGER and PEACOCK make one more attempt each to obtain GREEN at rates they consider fair, but the others decline to part with it. UNICORN, recognizing that BLUE is broadly seen as least valuable and that RED is the color they want most, submits a final, slightly generous offer using BLUE to buy RED; PEACOCK, seeing a marginal dollar gain and wanting not to leave money on the table in the final opportunity, accepts once more.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips alongside common-value green chips. All players start with an equal inventory (10 of each color), so bargaining is about exploiting comparative advantages rather than correcting initial imbalances. Dog and Owl both value blue highly ($1.00) and red moderately/low ($0.80 and $0.30), while Mouse values red very low ($0.20) and blue modestly ($0.60). Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Efficient trades tend to move red chips away from Mouse toward Dog, and blue chips away from Mouse toward Dog or Owl, while green can serve as a neutral medium or sweetener because its value is symmetric. However, each participant only sees their own values and must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances, creating uncertainty about partner valuations and willingness to trade. The random tie-breaking when multiple players accept a proposal means proposers cannot perfectly target their preferred partner; they must design proposals that are at least weakly attractive to more than one person or sufficiently tailored to a likely single accepter. Given the personas, Dog is cooperative, generous, and unlikely to reject small but positive gains; Mouse is cooperative with low rejection thresholds but somewhat strategic; Owl is numerate and moderately strategic with low rejection thresholds. Over three rounds, Dog’s repeated early-proposer role gives her multiple chances to test the market and signal cooperative intent. Mouse and Owl will likely accept any strictly value-increasing trade with modest fairness, and propose offers that give others some benefit while capturing more surplus themselves. Because of the limited three-round horizon, players will not fully learn others’ valuations; instead, they will experiment with plausible win-win red/blue swaps and occasionally use green as a balancing chip. We forecast mostly small, clearly positive-sum accepted trades and few outright rejections once a trade offers more value to the responder than it costs them, with some conservative shading in early-round proposals due to uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color and know only their own values. Dog moves first and, given her cooperative profile and strong value for blue, is likely to test a modest, clearly win-win offer aimed at acquiring blue or green from someone who might value red or green differently. Mouse and Owl will be cautious but inclined to accept strictly profitable trades. Because no one yet knows others’ valuations, offers will be conservative and exploratory, with small sizes and symmetric-looking trades. Over the three turns of this round, we expect initial feeler offers, at least one profitable acceptance, and emerging clues about who values what.

Going into round 1, several trades have occurred, and each player now has partial information about others’ preferences: Mouse’s willingness to give up blue and red for modest gain hints that they value those colors less, and Owl’s acceptance of trades gaining red and giving green suggests they value red more than green at the margin. Dog has successfully increased her blue and adjusted red/green balances, consistent with her high cooperation and desire for blue. With some beliefs updated, proposals now become more targeted: Dog will focus on acquiring more blue or green in ways that remain win-win; Mouse will try to offload low-value red or maybe blue for green or small gains; Owl will continue to exploit red/green differences. However, uncertainty about exact marginal valuations remains, so players still propose modest-size, clearly positive-sum trades rather than aggressive exploitation.

By round 2, the final round, inventories are more differentiated, and players have sharper though still imperfect beliefs about comparative advantages: Dog and Owl both seem to prize blue and are willing to pay with red or green; Mouse is comfortable parting with red and some blue in exchange for green or slightly better-valued chips. The horizon is short—only three turns remain—so players will be more willing to take any small strictly positive gains without worrying about future signaling. Proposers will try to convert remaining low-value chips into higher-value ones based on their private valuations, and responders with low rejection thresholds will accept any trade that clearly increases their own dollar payoff. We expect nearly all feasible, mutually beneficial proposals in this final round to be accepted, with deals focused on cleaning up residual inefficiencies in red/blue holdings using green or red as payment.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips and common-valued green chips ($0.50 each). All three players start symmetric in quantities (10 of each color) but asymmetric in values. Rabbit-yellow-6681 values red chips extremely highly (RED=$1.00) and blue moderately (BLUE=$0.30); buffalo-purple-4069 values red moderately (RED=$0.50) and blue poorly (BLUE=$0.20); alligator-orange-6315 values red slightly more than green (RED=$0.60 vs GREEN=$0.50) and blue equal to green (BLUE=$0.50). Greens are a safe, middle-value asset for everyone.

The key surplus-creating directions are: (1) red should flow toward rabbit (who values it at $1.00) from alligator ($0.60) and especially buffalo ($0.50); (2) blue should flow toward alligator ($0.50) from buffalo ($0.20) and rabbit ($0.30); (3) green can serve as neutral payment since all value it equally. However, players do not know each others’ exact values, only their own plus the public ranges, so they infer others’ preferences from observed trades. The UI surplus calculator ensures that no one will knowingly accept a strictly negative expected-value trade for themselves, but they may accept very small gains if they are tolerant of low margins.

Persona priors imply that alligator is comfortable with extreme, self-favoring splits and rarely rejects, suggesting he will probe for aggressively favorable deals, especially trading away greens or blues for red or more valuable colors in his belief. Buffalo is more fairness-minded and somewhat rejection-prone; she is willing to share surplus but also to reject offers that feel too lopsided or risky. Rabbit is numerate and strategic, very tolerant of low payoffs to herself in ultimatum, and moderately generous as a proposer, suggesting she may accept even mildly beneficial deals and may at times overpay to secure high-value red chips.

Given the random recipient rule when multiple players accept, proposers face an externality: a generous offer to attract one specific player might also attract a less-desired counterpart. This tends to push offers toward narrower margins or toward structures that target the likely high-value counterpart (e.g., trades that are attractive mainly to the player likely to value a chip color more). Over three rounds, each player has three proposal turns. The likely pattern is that early offers are exploratory, with small quantities to learn who is responsive. As histories accumulate, players adjust: alligator tries to accumulate red and blue at good prices; buffalo looks for fair, moderate-gain trades, possibly dumping blue for green or red; rabbit tries hard to acquire red, even if she must pay in blue or green, and will accept any strictly beneficial trade involving red. In equilibrium-like play, most accepted trades will involve red flowing toward rabbit and blue flowing toward alligator, with green and less-valued colors used as payment. Uncertainty about others’ valuations and the random-recipient mechanism limit how aggressively any one player can exploit their comparative advantage, leading to some declined offers and cautious small-size trades.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 chips of each color and have no information about others’ valuations beyond the public ranges. They are likely to use small, low-risk offers to probe who values which color. Alligator moves first and will test trading away a color he values slightly less for something he suspects might be more valuable. Buffalo will likely seek a fair, modestly profitable trade, possibly reducing low-value blue in exchange for green or red. Rabbit will look for any profitable way to gain red while giving up blue or, if needed, green, but may keep quantities small until she sees how others respond.

By round 1, observed trades reveal patterns. Alligator has shown willingness to trade away blue to obtain red; buffalo has shown that she is eager to convert low-value blue into more valuable green or red; rabbit has signaled an appetite for red even at a small green cost. Now holdings are asymmetric, and players can update beliefs: buffalo infers that both others may value red more than blue; rabbit infers that buffalo is relatively willing to part with red when compensated; alligator suspects that buffalo is an easy partner for blue-for-red trades. Proposers now try slightly larger or more targeted trades, using green as a stabilizing medium-value chip when necessary.

In the final round, players are aware that no future trading opportunities exist, so there is no reason to hold back profitable trades. Rabbit is strongly motivated to grab remaining red from others if she can pay with blue, which is relatively low-value for both her and especially for buffalo. Alligator, who likes both red and blue at or above green’s value, may still seek small, sure-profit trades but will avoid giving up red cheaply once he suspects rabbit prizes it. Buffalo’s priority is to shed remaining blue for anything of higher value and to avoid ending the game with too many low-value blue chips. Time pressure and clearer belief alignment make acceptance more likely for modestly positive deals, but grossly unfavorable proposals are still declined.
